summary: The influence of the low-frequency gravitational waves coupled with electromagnetic waves in material media on the test masses is investigated. The propagation of coupled gravitational waves in rarefied gases and cold magnetized plasma is considered. It has been shown that under specific conditions the amplitude of the coupled gravitational waves in a media reaches values of the order of the amplitude of transverse gravitational waves from external astrophysical sources. The specific properties of longitudinal gravitational waves coupled with electromagnetic waves in a medium in relation to transverse gravitational waves from external sources are considered as well.

AI.tldr: The paper explores the interaction between low-frequency gravitational waves and electromagnetic waves in materials like rarefied gases and magnetized plasma, showing that under certain conditions their amplitudes can rival those from external astrophysical sources.
AI.motivation: To understand how coupled gravitational and electromagnetic waves behave in different media and their potential detectability compared to external gravitational wave sources.
AI.method: The study investigates the propagation of coupled gravitational-electromagnetic waves in specific media (rarefied gases and cold magnetized plasma), analyzing their amplitude under particular conditions.
AI.result: The research found that coupled gravitational waves' amplitudes in media can reach comparable levels to transverse gravitational waves from external astrophysical sources under specific conditions.
AI.conclusion: Longitudinal gravitational waves coupled with electromagnetic waves in media exhibit distinct properties from transverse waves, offering new insights into detection methods and astrophysical applications.

title: The ultrafine splitting of heavy quarkonium with next-to-next-to-next-to-next-to-leading-order accuracy
summary: We compute the hyperfine splitting of P-wave heavy quarkonium states with next-to-next-to-next-to-next-to-leading-order accuracy. The resummation of logarithms with next-to-next-to-next-to-next-to-leading-logarithmic accuracy is also addressed. A phenomenological analysis of these results is performed for bottomonium, charmonium and the $B_c$ system. We also apply these results to positronium, muonium, hydrogen and muonic hydrogen.

AI.tldr: The paper calculates the hyperfine splitting in P-wave heavy quarkonium states with high precision (N4LO), including advanced logarithm resummation (N3LL), and applies the results to various systems including bottomonium, charmonium, Bc mesons, positronium, muonium, hydrogen, and muonic hydrogen.
AI.motivation: To achieve unprecedented accuracy in predicting hyperfine splitting in heavy quarkonium systems and extend the analysis to other bound states for broader validation and application.
AI.method: The study employs next-to-next-to-next-to-next-to-leading-order (N4LO) perturbative calculations combined with next-to-next-to-next-to-leading-logarithmic (N3LL) resummation techniques for logarithmic terms. Phenomenological analyses are performed across multiple systems.
AI.result: High-precision predictions for hyperfine splitting in bottomonium, charmonium, Bc systems, and other bound states like positronism. The results improve theoretical predictions and may resolve discrepancies with experimental data.
AI.conclusion: The N4LO+N3LL framework significantly enhances accuracy in hyperfine splitting calculations, demonstrating applicability across a wide range of quarkonium and leptonium systems, which strengthens the theoretical foundation for precision tests in particle physics.

title: HAWC J0630+186 Could Not Be Powered by PSR J0630+19
summary: 3HWC J0630+186 is one of the very-high-energy gamma-ray sources in the third High-Altitude Water Cherenkov (HAWC) catalog, its origin and source are, however, not clearly identified. The only possible associated source is PSR J0630+19 depart from the center of 3HWC J0630+186. A few TeV halos of pulsars are currently believed the most dominant TeV-PeV gamma-ray sources, and PSR J0630+19 was firstly discovered by Arecibo survey with normal pulsar period, but its age and spin-down luminosity are not available. It is then difficult to determine if 3HWC J0630+186 and PSR J0630+19 are associated or not. With the awarded telescope time in five-hundred-meter aperture spherical radio telescope (FAST) observing cycle, we have obtained the follow-up timing observations of PSR J0630+19 with observed duration more than one year. From our pulsar data analysis, we determined a more precise position and derived parameters via pulsar timing. The parameters indicate that it is an old pulsar with energy loss too low to power the very-high-energy emissions from 3HWC J0630+186.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates the association between the unidentified very-high-energy gamma-ray source 3HWC J0630+186 and the nearby pulsar PSR J0630+19. FAST timing observations revealed PSR J0630+19 is an old pulsar with insufficient spin-down energy to power the observed gamma-ray emissions, suggesting the source is likely unrelated and could represent a new TeV-PeV emitter.
AI.motivation: To determine whether 3HWC J0630+186 is associated with PSR J0630+19, as their positions are offset and prior pulsar parameters were insufficient for verification.
AI.method: Used FAST telescope timing observations over a year to obtain precise pulsar parameters, including position and spin-down luminosity.
AI.result: PSR J0630+19 is an old pulsar with low energy loss, incapable of powering the detected very-high-energy gamma rays. This rules out the pulsar as the source of 3HWC J0630+186.
AI.conclusion: 3HWC J0630+186 likely originates from an independent TeV-PeV source, highlighting the need for further studies on alternative explanations like supernova remnants or other astrophysical objects.

title: Characterizing the Instrumental Profile of LAMOST
summary: The instrumental profile (IP) of a telescope is of great significance for spectroscopic analyses, especially for wavelength calibration and stellar parameter measurements. The Large Sky Area Multi-Object Fiber Spectroscopic Telescope (LAMOST) employs arc lamps for wavelength calibration. These lamps produce sharp emission lines with known wavelengths, and the observed arc lamp spectra can well characterize the IP. However, IPs are influenced by multiple factors, making them difficult to model accurately with traditional methods. Neural networks, which can automatically capture complex patterns and nonlinear features in data, provide a promising approach for high-precision IP measurement. We therefore construct a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) based on The Payne neural network to derive IPs for LAMOST. After training, the model can retrieve the IP for any fiber, at any wavelength, and at any time. We then apply the derived IP to stellar radial velocity (RV) measurements and analyze the impact of different IP center localization methods on the results. Finally, the dispersion of the measured RVs is reduced by approximately 3 km/s. This improvement will facilitate the search for long-period binary stars via RV variations.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a neural network approach using an MLP based on The Payne architecture to accurately model the instrumental profile (IP) of LAMOST, improving stellar radial velocity measurements by reducing RV dispersion by ~3 km/s.
AI.motivation: Traditional methods struggle to accurately model LAMOST's IP due to its dependence on multiple complex factors, necessitating a more robust approach for precise wavelength calibration and stellar parameter measurements.
AI.method: The authors construct a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) based on The Payne neural network to derive IPs. The model is trained using arc lamp spectra, enabling IP retrieval for any fiber, wavelength, and time. The derived IP is then applied to stellar radial velocity measurements, testing different center localization methods.
AI.result: Applying the neural network-derived IP reduced the dispersion of measured radial velocities by approximately 3 km/s, enhancing precision for detecting long-period binary stars through RV variations.
AI.conclusion: The neural network model effectively captures the complex patterns in LAMOST's IP, offering a significant improvement over traditional methods and providing a valuable tool for future astrophysical studies requiring high-precision RV measurements.

title: Black Holes Surrounded by Perfect Fluid Dark Matter in Eddington-inspired Born-Infeld Gravity
summary: In this work, we exactly derive the solution for the gravitational field of a black hole in Eddington-inspired Born-Infeld (EiBI) gravity, surrounded by perfect fluid dark matter. We analyze how the event horizon and the black hole dimensions vary as a function of the model parameters, exploring the fundamental properties of this spacetime. Through numerical investigations, we examine the geodesics of massive particles and demonstrate the high sensitivity of stable circular orbits to the system's coupling constants.

AI.tldr: The paper derives the exact solution for the gravitational field of a black hole in EiBI gravity with surrounding perfect fluid dark matter, analyzes changes in event horizon and black hole dimensions with model parameters, and numerically examines particle geodesics showing sensitivity of stable orbits to coupling constants.
AI.motivation: Investigate non-singular gravity theories like EiBI to address black hole singularities and explore dark matter effects using perfect fluid models.
AI.method: Exact derivation of EiBI black hole solutions with dark matter, parameter analysis of event horizons, and numerical simulations of geodesics for massive particles.
AI.result: Found that black hole properties and stable orbits are highly sensitive to EiBI coupling constants, offering insights into non-singular black hole dynamics and dark matter interactions.
AI.conclusion: EiBI gravity with perfect fluid dark matter provides a viable framework to study singularity-free black holes and observable effects through orbital dynamics.

title: Linking Axions, the Flavor Problem, and Neutrino Masses through a Flavored Peccei-Quinn Symmetry
summary: Recent measurements by several experimental collaborations have reported deviations from Standard Model (SM) predictions in diphoton final states, potentially hinting at the existence of intermediate scalar resonances above the electroweak scale. Such anomalies can be naturally accommodated within SM extensions featuring an enlarged scalar sector. In particular, multi-Higgs doublet frameworks arise in Flavored Axion Models (FAMs), which have been proposed to explain the texture zeros of quark mass matrices. These models provide a unified description of quark masses and the Cabibbo-Kobayashi-Maskawa (CKM) mixing matrix while simultaneously addressing the strong CP problem. In this work we study a concrete FAM realization augmented with Majorana masses for right-handed neutrinos, implementing a type-I seesaw mechanism. In this model the flavor structure is effectively determined by the vacuum expectation values of the scalar doublets and Yukawa couplings of order one. Within this framework, neutrino and axion mass scales are intrinsically connected, as the heavy right-handed neutrinos obtain their masses from the scalar field responsible for the spontaneous breaking of the Peccei-Quinn symmetry. We further explore the phenomenological implications of the model, including constraints from flavor-changing neutral currents derived from semileptonic decays, as well as current experimental limits on the axion-photon coupling obtained from axion search experiments.

AI.tldr: This paper explores a Flavored Axion Model (FAM) with Majorana neutrinos, addressing quark mass matrices, CKM mixing, strong CP problem, and linking neutrino/axion masses. It examines constraints from flavor physics and axion searches.
AI.motivation: To explain texture zeros in quark mass matrices, unify quark masses/CKM mixing, solve the strong CP problem, and address diphoton anomalies via scalar resonances beyond the Standard Model.
AI.method: Studying a FAM with multi-Higgs doublets and right-handed neutrinos implementing type-I seesaw. The model's flavor structure depends on scalar VEVs and O(1) Yukawa couplings. Analyzes phenomenological constraints like FCNCs and axion-photon coupling limits.
AI.result: Neutrino and axion mass scales are intrinsically linked through Peccei-Quinn symmetry breaking. Model parameters are constrained by existing experimental data from flavor physics and axion detection experiments.
AI.conclusion: The model provides a comprehensive framework connecting flavor structure, dark matter (axions), neutrino masses, and potential scalar resonances. Future experiments could test predictions regarding axion couplings and collider signatures of scalar states.

title: Searching for Black Hole Candidates in Quiescence by Using Multi-band Observations in Globular Cluster M22 (NGC6656)
comment: 10 pages, 4 figures, 1 table, to be submitted
summary: We present a multi-wavelength investigation of radio sources in the globular cluster M22 (NGC6656) using VLA, HST, and Chandra observations. Among the eight identified counterparts, we highlight VLA22 as the most promising stellar-mass black hole (BH) candidate. Its radio and X-ray luminosities follow the established $L_{R}-L_{X}$ correlation for quiescent black hole low-mass X-ray binaries (BH-LMXBs), while its moderately steep radio spectrum and X-ray spectral hardening further support this classification. Analysis of two potential optical counterparts-a bright main-sequence star and a faint subgiant/red giant-suggests a binary system with a relatively long orbital period. The discovery of VLA22 consistent with recent retention models that stellar-mass BH can be retained within globular clusters over Hubble timescales. Additionally, VLA19 exhibits a characteristically inverted radio spectrum ($α= 0.79 \pm 0.39, S_ν\propto ν^α$) indicative of a compact jet, while VLA40 also aligns with the BH $L_{R}-L_{X}$ track, though both require further observations to definitively confirm their nature.

AI.tldr: This study identifies potential stellar-mass black holes in globular cluster M22 using multi-wavelength data. VLA22 shows strong evidence as a black hole candidate through its radio-X-ray correlation and binary system characteristics, supporting BH retention models. Other sources like VLA19 and VLA40 suggest possible black holes but need more data.
AI.motivation: To investigate retention mechanisms of stellar-mass black holes in globular clusters and identify new BH candidates through multi-wavelength analysis.
AI.method: Combined VLA (radio), HST (optical), and Chandra (X-ray) observations were used to analyze radio sources in M22. Candidates were evaluated via L_R-L_X correlations, spectral analysis, and optical/X-ray variability.
AI.result: VLA22 strongly matches BH-LMXB characteristics with confirmed L_R-L_X correlation and binary system indicators. VLA19 shows inverted spectrum suggestive of jet activity, while VLA40 aligns with BH tracks but both need confirmation. Results support long-term BH retention in clusters.
AI.conclusion: Multi-wavelength observations confirm viable BH retention in globular clusters. New candidates expand known BH populations in dense environments, emphasizing need for continued monitoring and additional data.

title: A Robust Geometric Distortion Solution for Main Survey Camera of CSST
summary: The advancement in sensitivity and field of view of next-generation wide-field survey telescopes requires astrometric measurements with high precision, even in the presence of significant geometric distortions. To address this challenge, we develop a Weighted Polynomial Distortion Correction in 2-Phase (WPDC-2P) method. This approach enhances stellar cross-matching, incorporates distance-based weighting into the traditional polynomial fitting, and employs a look-up table to absorb the remaining distortion residuals. Validated on simulated data from the Main Survey Camera of the \emph{Chinese Space Station Survey Telescope} (CSST), incorporating geometric distortions up to approximately $200$ pixels, the method achieves astrometric standard deviation ranging from 0.013 to 0.107 pixels (0.03 pixels for the $g$-1 detector) across all 18 detectors. Under extreme crowding conditions (e.g., globular cluster NGC 2298), the astrometric precision for the $g$-1 detector reaches 0.05-pixel level within the central region ($r_d < 4000$), despite a centroiding precision of $\sim$0.04 pixels. When applied to the Beijing-Arizona Sky Survey data, for which the standard pipeline delivers an astrometric uncertainty of $\sim$20 mas, our method reduces the positional scatter to $ σ_{Δα}=5.494$ mas (0.01 pixels) and $ σ_{Δδ}=9.981$ mas (0.02 pixels) using only a weighted 3rd-order polynomial correction. The method has been integrated into the CSST data processing pipeline and is prepared for further refinement using on-orbit calibration data.

AI.tldr: The WPDC-2P method improves astrometric precision in high-distortion environments using weighted polynomial fitting and a look-up table, achieving sub-pixel accuracy in both simulated CSST data and ground-based surveys.
AI.motivation: Next-generation telescopes require high-precision astrometry despite significant geometric distortions, necessitating a more robust correction method.
AI.method: Develops WPDC-2P with distance-based weighted polynomial fitting in two phases, incorporating a look-up table for residual distortion correction.
AI.result: Achieved astrometric standard deviations of 0.013-0.107 pixels in CSST simulations and reduced positional scatter to 5.5-10 mas in Beijing-Arizona data under extreme crowding.
AI.conclusion: WPDC-2P effectively addresses geometric distortion challenges, integrated into CSST pipelines, and ready for on-orbit calibration refinements.

title: Dual Cutler-Vallisneri Corrections: Mitigating PSD Drift in Zero-Latency Gravitational-Wave Searches
comment: 13 pages, 4 figures. Prepared for submission to Physical Review D
summary: Maximizing pre-merger warning times in gravitational-wave searches requires minimizing algorithmic latency. While current pipelines typically rely on truncated linear-phase filters, minimum-phase whitening offers a zero-latency alternative that eliminates the acausal look-ahead buffer. However, this causal approach exposes the analysis to spectral drift, where the whitening operator applied to live data diverges from the static template bank, creating a functional perturbation of the matched-filter metric. We develop a perturbative framework generalizing the Cutler-Vallisneri formalism to address these metric errors, deriving analytic expressions for the resulting timing, phase, and SNR biases. Validated against exact stationary-phase models and numerical injections, these corrections achieve $<1\%$ error. Applying this framework to GWTC-4.0 events with realistic 1-week power spectral density (PSD) lags, we find that uncorrected drift induces severe systematics: detector-pair timing biases exceeding $200 μ$s, phase shifts up to 0.2 rad, and sky-localization errors of $5^{\circ}-10^{\circ}$. Additionally, we observe a median signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) loss of $3-5\%$, with outliers exceeding $8\%$. These results demonstrate that while minimum-phase whitening maximizes the early-warning window, analytic drift corrections are essential to maintain detection volume and pointing accuracy in future observing runs.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the issue of spectral drift in minimum-phase whitening for gravitational-wave searches, developing a perturbative framework to correct timing, phase, and SNR biases caused by PSD lags. It shows significant systematic errors when uncorrected and emphasizes the necessity of these corrections for maintaining detection accuracy while maximizing early warnings.
AI.motivation: To minimize algorithmic latency in gravitational-wave searches for maximizing pre-merger warning times, minimum-phase whitening is used, but it introduces spectral drift issues. The motivation is to develop a method to correct the resulting metric errors to preserve detection accuracy without sacrificing latency.
AI.method: The authors generalize the Cutler-Vallisneri formalism to create a perturbative framework addressing metric errors from spectral drift. They derive analytic expressions for timing, phase, and SNR biases and validate these using stationary-phase models and numerical injections. Application to GWTC-4.0 events with 1-week PSD lags is performed.
AI.result: Without correction, PSD lags cause timing biases over 200 μs, phase shifts up to 0.2 rad, sky localization errors of 5°-10°, and SNR loss of 3-5% (up to 8% in outliers). The proposed corrections achieve <1% error, showing their effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: Minimum-phase whitening is critical for zero-latency early warnings, but analytic drift corrections are essential to maintain detection volume and pointing accuracy in future gravitational-wave observing runs.

title: Scalar contributions to the S, T, U parameters in a 3-3-1 model
summary: Electroweak precision tests, expressed through the oblique parameters $S$, $T$, and $U$, impose stringent constraints on physics beyond the Standard Model. Gauge extensions of the Standard Model based on the $SU(3)_L \times U(1)_N$ symmetry predict a rich scalar and gauge spectrum that contribute to these parameters. Previous studies have shown that 3-3-1 gauge bosons give negligible contributions to the oblique parameters, while the contributions of the scalar sector to these parameters have received comparatively little attention. In particular, for the version of the $SU(3)_L \times U(1)_N$ model with right-handed neutrinos, the impact of the scalar sector on $S$, $T$ , and $U$ has not yet been addressed. In this work, we fill this gap and address sistematically the scalar contributions to the $S$, $T$ and $U$ within this version. As main result, we show that the parameter $T$ put stringent constraints on the masses and energy scales associated to the spectrum of scalars of the model.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates scalar contributions to electroweak oblique parameters (S, T, U) in a 3-3-1 model with right-handed neutrinos, finding that parameter T imposes strict constraints on scalar masses and energy scales.
AI.motivation: To address the previously unstudied impact of scalar sectors on oblique parameters in the SU(3)L×U(1)N model with right-handed neutrinos, which is crucial for testing beyond-Standard-Model physics through electroweak precision tests.
AI.method: Systematic analysis of scalar contributions to S, T, and U parameters within the specified 3-3-1 model framework, focusing on the previously neglected scalar sector's effects.
AI.result: Parameter T imposes stringent constraints on scalar particle masses and energy scales in the model, while S and U contributions are relatively minor or previously considered.
AI.conclusion: Scalar sector analysis is essential for validating 3-3-1 models against electroweak precision data, with parameter T being the key constraint for model parameter spaces.

title: Low-energy atmospheric neutrino flux calculation with accelerator-data-driven tuning
summary:
We have incorporated a hadron interaction tuning based on accelerator data into our atmospheric neutrino flux calculation, which has been used to analyze atmospheric neutrino oscillations at Super-Kamiokande. This new approach enables a more direct evaluation of the flux uncertainty than a conventional tuning using atmospheric muons.
  The neutrino flux calculated with this new tuning is 5\%--10\% smaller but still consistent with our previously published prediction within its uncertainty. The flavor ratio $(ν_μ+\barν_μ)/(ν_e+\barν_e)$ and $\barν/ν$ ratios were consistent with the previous prediction. Based on the measurement errors of the accelerator data, we evaluated the flux uncertainty associated with the new tuning to be 7\%--9\% in the $E_ν <$ 1 GeV region, which was difficult to assess with the conventional tuning. The flux uncertainty in the $1<E_ν<10$ GeV region was evaluated to be 5\%--7\%, which is an improvement over the conventional tuning.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a novel atmospheric neutrino flux calculation method using accelerator data to tune hadron interactions, reducing flux estimates by 5-10% compared to prior work. Uncertainties are better quantified at low energies (7-9% for Eν <1 GeV) and mid-range energies (5-7% for 1-10 GeV), improving upon conventional muon-based tuning methods.
AI.motivation: To improve the accuracy and uncertainty quantification of atmospheric neutrino flux calculations, addressing limitations of previous methods reliant on atmospheric muons which struggled to assess flux uncertainties, especially at low energies.
AI.method: Incorporated accelerator-derived hadron interaction data into the neutrino flux model. This new tuning approach allows direct evaluation of flux uncertainties, unlike conventional methods using atmospheric muons. The authors calculated flux values and compared uncertainties across energy ranges (Eν <1 GeV and 1-10 GeV).
AI.result: The new flux predictions are 5-10% lower than previous estimates but remain within their uncertainty ranges. Flavor ratios ((νμ+ν̄μ)/(νe+ν̄e) and ν̄/ν) match prior results. Flux uncertainties are quantified as 7-9% (Eν <1 GeV) and 5-7% (1-10 GeV), surpassing conventional tuning precision.
AI.conclusion: The accelerator-based tuning provides a superior method for estimating atmospheric neutrino flux uncertainties across energy ranges, particularly enhancing low-energy region accuracy. This advancement supports more reliable neutrino oscillation analyses at experiments like Super-Kamiokande.

title: The Key to Unlocking Exoplanet Biosignatures: a UK-led IR Spectrograph for the Habitable Worlds Observatory Coronagraph
comment: White paper submitted to the UK Space Agency's initiative "UK Space Frontiers 2035" https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/uk-space-frontiers-2035-astro-planetary-and-helio
summary: The detection of life on rocky exoplanets in the habitable zones of nearby stars would be a paradigm-shifting advance, and it is one of the greatest scientific challenges of our time. There is no single spectral feature that is an unambiguous sign of life on a given exoplanet. Instead, the current state-of-the-art approach involves detecting multiple molecular atmospheric features that should not exist together in equilibrium, e.g. simultaneous detection of O$_2$ and CH$_4$. Spectra across a wide wavelength (0.3-1.7 $μ$m) range are necessary to cover multiple spectral features per molecule of interest and to contextualise the suite of molecular features detected. While the US will lead the optical arm of the Habitable Worlds Observatory (HWO) coronagraph, a UK-led contribution of a near-infrared Integral Field Spectrograph (IFS) for the infrared arm will ensure UK leadership in the flagship scientific goal of HWO - to search for signatures of life on potentially habitable exoplanets.

AI.tldr: The UK-led near-infrared Integral Field Spectrograph (IFS) for the HWO's infrared arm will enhance efforts to detect biosignatures on exoplanets by providing comprehensive spectral data across a wide wavelength range, complementing US-led optical efforts.
AI.motivation: Detecting biosignatures (like coexisting O₂ and CH₄) on exoplanets requires observing multiple non-equilibrium atmospheric features across broad wavelengths, necessitating collaboration and advanced instruments.
AI.method: The IFS will capture spectra from 0.3-1.7 μm, covering multiple features per molecule to contextualize detections, ensuring UK leadership in the HWO's flagship goal of searching for life signs.
AI.result: Enhanced capability to identify non-equilibrium atmospheric compositions indicative of life, advancing the HWO's mission through combined UK-US instrumentation.
AI.conclusion: This collaboration underscores the necessity of international partnerships and advanced instruments like the IFS to achieve groundbreaking discoveries in exoplanet biosignature detection.

title: Gravitational waveforms and accretion characteristics in a quantum-corrected black hole without Cauchy horizons
summary: The use of physical phenomena in the strong-field regime has become a primarily methodology for probing quantum-corrected gravity. This paper investigates periodic orbits, gravitational waves, and accretion disk radiation for a quantum-corrected black hole without Cauchy horizons. First, by analyzing the trajectory equations of massive particles in the equatorial plane, we study the influence of the quantum parameter $ζ$ on the stability of circular orbits. The results show that an increase in $ζ$ leads to an outward migration of both the innermost stable circular orbit and the marginally bound orbit, accompanied by an increase in the required specific angular momentum for particle motion on these two orbits. Then, we further investigate the periodic orbit characteristics of particles and compute the associated gravitational waveforms for extreme mass-ratio inspirals. It is demonstrated that quantum corrections induce a cumulative phase shift in the gravitational wave signal, leading to significant dephasing compared to the classical Schwarzschild case. Furthermore, based on the Novikov-Thorne thin accretion disk model, we evaluate the radiation characteristics of the accretion disk around this quantum-corrected black hole. The results indicate that the introduction of the quantum parameter suppresses the radiant energy flux, effective temperature, and overall radiative efficiency of the disk. These distinctive dynamical and radiative deviations provide potential phenomenological support for distinguishing quantum-corrected geometries from classical black holes using multiple observational means in the future.

AI.tldr: The paper explores effects of quantum corrections on black holes by examining orbits, gravitational waves, and accretion disk radiation. Key findings include outward shifts in stable orbits, altered gravitational wave phases, and reduced disk radiation efficiency due to quantum parameter ζ, offering observational tools to differentiate quantum-corrected black holes from classical models.
AI.motivation: To probe quantum-corrected gravity through strong-field phenomena, examining how quantum corrections modify black hole dynamics and observables compared to classical Schwarzschild solutions.
AI.method: Used trajectory equations for massive particles in the equatorial plane, analyzed periodic orbits, simulated gravitational waveforms for extreme mass-ratio inspirals, and applied the Novikov-Thorne accretion disk model to evaluate radiation properties with quantum parameter ζ.
AI.result: Quantum parameter ζ increases the innermost stable circular orbit and marginally bound orbit radii, enhances required particle angular momentum, induces phase shifts in gravitational waves causing dephasing, and reduces accretion disk's energy flux, temperature, and radiative efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The observed dynamical and radiative deviations due to ζ provide phenomenological signatures to distinguish quantum-corrected black holes from classical ones via future multi-observation methods.

title: Dark matter trio in classically conformal theories: WIMP, supercooling, and monopole
comment: 8 pages + 4 figures + appendix + references. Comments are welcome
summary: Beyond solving the hierarchy problem, classically conformal (CC) theories naturally accommodate dark matter (DM). In this work, we explore the CC $SU(2)_X$ gauge theory with a triplet dark scalar, uncovering three distinct DM scenarios: WIMP, supercooled DM, and monopole. The production mechanisms are strongly influenced by the CC model's unique first-order phase transition evolution history, which differs significantly from those in non-conformal models. We obtain the viable parameter space for each scenario and investigate the current constraints and future sensitivities at terrestrial experiments and gravitational wave observatories.

AI.tldr: This paper explores classically conformal SU(2)_X gauge theories with a triplet dark scalar, proposing three dark matter scenarios (WIMP, supercooled DM, monopole) influenced by the model's unique phase transition history, and examines their viability and experimental constraints.
AI.motivation: To extend beyond solving the hierarchy problem by using CC theories to naturally incorporate dark matter, leveraging the unique phase transition properties for diverse DM scenarios.
AI.method: Analysis of CC SU(2)_X gauge theory with triplet scalar DM, focusing on first-order phase transitions to derive production mechanisms and parameter space for three DM scenarios. Constraints from terrestrial experiments and gravitational wave observatories are evaluated.
AI.result: Identified viable parameter spaces for WIMP, supercooled DM, and monopole scenarios. Highlights distinct impacts of first-order phase transitions compared to non-conformal models. Assessments of current experimental constraints and future detection prospects.
AI.conclusion: CC models offer promising dark matter frameworks with testable predictions. Future experiments (terrestrial/gravitational wave) can critically test these scenarios, emphasizing the role of phase transitions in cosmological evolution.

title: Beyond Fermi-II: Intermittent Particle Acceleration by Relativistic Turbulence in Astrophysical Plasmas
comment: Proceedings of the High Energy Phenomena in Relativistic Outflows IX (HEPRO-IX) conference, 4-8 August 2025, Rio de Janeiro, Brazil. PoS(HEPRO-IX)040
summary: Stochastic particle acceleration in turbulent plasmas plays a key role in shaping high-energy emission from relativistic outflows, such as those in Active Galactic Nuclei (AGN) and microquasars. While traditional Fermi-II models provide a foundational framework, they often oversimplify the complex nature of realistic magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) turbulence, especially in high-amplitude ($δB/B_0 \sim 1$) and relativistic regimes. Recent plasma simulations for these conditions have revealed highly non-linear energization effects, such as sudden, large momentum jumps, that remain largely unexplored in astrophysical applications. We present a novel Monte Carlo framework STRIPE that models particle acceleration as a continuous-time random walk (CTRW), capturing both intermittent energy gains and radiative losses. The stochastic evolution of particle momenta is driven by jumps with random magnitudes determined by a distribution of magnetic-field-line velocity gradients, with synchrotron and inverse Compton cooling incorporated self-consistently. Using STRIPE, we explore particle acceleration under physical conditions characteristic of TeV-PeV $γ$-ray emitting microquasars recently identified by Large High Altitude Air Shower Observatory (LHAASO). We find that relativistic, high-amplitude turbulence naturally produces particle spectra with steep low-energy cutoffs, and hard extended power-law high-energy tails reaching tens of PeV. These features differ markedly from standard quasi-linear theory and are well suited to explaining the unexpectedly hard TeV-PeV spectra of LHAASO-detected microquasars. These results highlight turbulent acceleration in the relativistic regime as a promising mechanism for particle energization in microquasar systems, as well as potentially other extreme astrophysical environments.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces STRIPE, a Monte Carlo framework modeling particle acceleration via continuous-time random walks in relativistic, high-amplitude turbulence, producing steep low-energy cutoffs and hard PeV-scale power-law spectra that explain TeV-PeV observations in microquasars.
AI.motivation: Traditional Fermi-II models oversimplify MHD turbulence effects in relativistic, high-amplitude regimes (δB/B₀ ~1), which recent simulations show exhibit complex energization like sudden momentum jumps. Existing theories fail to explain the observed hard TeV-PeV spectra from microquasars detected by LHAASO.
AI.method: Develops STRIPE: a CTRW Monte Carlo model incorporating random momentum jumps from MHD turbulence velocity gradients and self-consistent radiative cooling (synchrotron/inverse Compton). Applied to microquasar conditions with relativistic turbulence.
AI.result: Produces particle spectra with steep low-energy cutoffs and hard power-law tails up to ~tens of PeV, differing from quasi-linear theory predictions and matching LHAASO observations. Demonstrates relativistic turbulence's efficacy in explaining extreme energy particle acceleration.
AI.conclusion: Relativistic high-amplitude turbulence via STRIPE explains microquasar TeV-PeV emission and suggests this mechanism's broader applicability in extreme astrophysical environments.

title: Accurate spectroscopic redshift estimation using non-negative matrix factorization: application to MUSE spectra
summary: Accurate and automated galaxy redshift determination is essential for maximizing the scientific return of spectroscopic surveys. In this paper, we propose a data-driven method to address this challenge. The method first learns a rest-frame representation of galaxy spectra using Non-negative Matrix Factorization (NMF). The method then reconstructs new spectra using this representation at different trial redshifts, and identifies the correct redshift by selecting the one that minimizes the reconstruction error. We apply our method to galaxy spectra from the Multi Unit Spectroscopic Explorer (MUSE), covering redshifts from 0 to 6.7. Our method achieves an overall success rate of 93.7%. We further demonstrate two applications: (i) the separation between true and false sources, and (ii) the detection of blended sources from one-dimensional spectra. Our results demonstrate that NMF-based representations provide a powerful and physically motivated framework for redshift estimation in current and future large spectroscopic surveys.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a data-driven method using Non-negative Matrix Factorization (NMF) to accurately determine galaxy redshifts by minimizing reconstruction errors of spectra at trial redshifts. It achieves a 93.7% success rate with applications in source separation and blended detection.
AI.motivation: Accurate and automated galaxy redshift determination is critical for maximizing the scientific output of spectroscopic surveys, especially as data volumes grow.
AI.method: The method employs NMF to create a rest-frame spectral representation, reconstructs spectra at various trial redshifts, and selects the redshift with the lowest reconstruction error. Applied to MUSE spectra covering redshifts 0–6.7.
AI.result: Achieved 93.7% success rate. Successfully separated true/false sources and detected blended sources from 1D spectra.
AI.conclusion: NMF-based redshift estimation offers a powerful, physically grounded approach for current/future large spectroscopic surveys.

title: Binary Black Hole inspirals cannot hide their eccentricity
summary: The events detected by the LIGO Virgo KAGRA collaboration over a period of 10 years have yielded a treasure trove of signals from compact binary coalescences. None of these events have shown a confident signature of eccentricity. With upgrades to the existing network and potential next generation gravitational wave detectors, we will be able to see much further into the universe increasing the likelihood of detecting eccentric systems. We improve upon the phenomenological approach of providing eccentricity constraints using an effective chirp mass model in the time frequency domain. We introduce an improved pixel collection method along with a likelihood based sampling approach inspired by Bayesian parameter estimation. Our approach constructs a likelihood from the product of energies collected across different eccentric harmonics in the time frequency representation. This formulation enables coarse but meaningful constraints on orbital eccentricity. Additionally, we incorporate information from the energy ratios between eccentric harmonics, further refining the eccentricity estimates. We test our approach on 500 non spinning equal mass eccentric systems and demonstrate that we can constrain the eccentricity within 0.2 around the true value. Moreover, our approach can deliver these constraints in 5 minutes on a machine with 50 cores. These results demonstrate that our phenomenological approach provides fast and reasonably accurate eccentricity estimates, making it a promising tool for rapid gravitational wave data analysis.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an improved method for constraining orbital eccentricity in gravitational wave signals from compact binary coalescences using an enhanced pixel collection and likelihood-based sampling approach in the time-frequency domain. It demonstrates effective eccentricity constraints within 0.2 accuracy and fast computation times, suitable for rapid data analysis.
AI.motivation: Despite 10 years of LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA observations yielding no confident eccentricity detections, upcoming detector upgrades increase the chance of finding eccentric systems. Current methods need improvement for efficient and accurate eccentricity estimation.
AI.method: The approach uses an effective chirp mass model with an improved pixel collection method in the time-frequency domain and a likelihood-based sampling inspired by Bayesian parameter estimation. The likelihood is constructed from energy products across eccentric harmonics, incorporating energy ratio information for refined estimates.
AI.result: Testing on 500 non-spinning, equal-mass eccentric systems showed eccentricity constraints within ±0.2 of true values, achieved in 5 minutes on a 50-core machine. This offers fast, reasonably accurate eccentricity estimation.
AI.conclusion: The phenomenological method provides a promising tool for rapid gravitational wave analysis, enabling timely eccentricity constraints as next-gen detectors come online.

title: A detailed analysis of possible new-physics effects in semileptonic decays $B_s \to D_s^{(*)}τ\barν$
summary: We study the semileptonic decays $B_s \to D_s^{(*)}τ\barν$ as a promising probe for new physics (NP) beyond the standard model (SM). The extension of the SM is done through the introduction of four-fermion operators beyond the $V-A$ structure with the corresponding Wilson coefficients characterizing their contribution. The constraints on these coefficients are obtained from recent experimental data. Form factors describing hadron transitions are calculated in our covariant quark model with infrared confinement. Theoretical predictions for the full set of observables in these channels are provided. We analyze possible NP effects to be tested in future experiments.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates the semileptonic decays B_s → D_s^{(*)}τν̄ as a probe for new physics beyond the Standard Model (SM). Using four-fermion operators with non-V−A structures and infrared-confinement-based form factors, they derive constraints on Wilson coefficients and predict observables for future experimental tests.
AI.motivation: To explore potential new physics contributions in B_s decays by extending the SM with four-fermion interactions, leveraging hadronic form factors calculated in a covariant quark model for precise predictions and experimental benchmarking.
AI.method: 1. Extend SM with four-fermion operators beyond V−A structure. 2. Calculate form factors using a covariant quark model incorporating infrared confinement. 3. Constrain NP Wilson coefficients using experimental data. 4. Predict observables for B_s → D_s^{(*)}τν̄ decays.
AI.result: Theoretical predictions for all relevant observables in these decay channels are provided, offering a framework to test new physics contributions against future experimental measurements.
AI.conclusion: The study highlights the potential of these decays as sensitive probes for physics beyond the SM, emphasizing the need for upcoming experimental results to validate or constrain the proposed new physics scenarios.

title: Temporal Variation of the Coronal Parameter in a Jetted Tidal Disruption Event: Swift J1644+57
comment: submitted to ApJ after revision, comments are welcome
summary: Tidal Disruption Events are exotic astrophysical phenomena where matter from a star or the interstellar medium is captured by a supermassive black hole. The process liberates enormous energy, within a few months to a year timescale, enough to detect dormant black holes in near as well as the farthest galaxies. We revisit the long-term spectral variabilities associated with the jetted Tidal Disruption Event \source~by exploring the archival X-ray data obtained with MAXI/GSC, Swift/XRT, and XMM-Newton observatories. Our analysis reveals that the spectral indices decrease non-monotonically as \source~evolves with time. We also find that the soft (0.3-1.5 keV) and hard (1.5-10 keV) X-ray photon counts are highly correlated with a maximum correlation coefficient of 0.95 and peak at {\it zero} lag. Moreover, the soft and hard band variabilities obtained from XMM-Newton observations are highly correlated with a Pearson cross-correlation coefficient of 0.96. This indicates that the soft and hard X-ray photons are emitted from the same site, which is most likely a Compton cloud, i.e., the corona. Assuming the hard X-ray photons originate from the corona, we find that the coronal parameter undergoes rapid expansion during the early phases when accompanied by a relativistic jet launching and subsequently evolves toward a state of saturation with minor fluctuations in the latter stages. The temporal variation of the coronal size is consistent with a simple theoretical conjecture. We also discuss the application of our analytical outcomes to other jetted and non-jetted tidal disruption events.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes spectral variabilities and correlations in X-ray data from a jetted Tidal Disruption Event (TDE) using datasets from MAXI/GSC, Swift/XRT, and XMM-Newton. It finds non-monotonic spectral index changes and strong correlations between soft and hard X-ray emissions, suggesting a common origin in a Compton cloud (corona). The corona expands rapidly early on with jet launch and later stabilizes. These findings align with theoretical models and implications for understanding jetted and non-jetted TDEs.
AI.motivation: To study long-term spectral variabilities in jetted TDEs and understand the origins of X-ray emissions, particularly the relationship between soft and hard bands and coronal dynamics.
AI.method: Analysis of archival X-ray data from MAXI/GSC, Swift/XRT, and XMM-Newton. Measured spectral indices, correlations between soft (0.3-1.5 keV) and hard (1.5-10 keV) X-ray counts, and temporal evolution of coronal parameters using cross-correlation coefficients and theoretical modeling.
AI.result: Non-monotonic spectral index decline; strong zero-lag correlation (0.95-0.96) between soft and hard X-ray counts, indicating same emission site (corona). Corona expands rapidly early during jet launching and stabilizes later, consistent with theoretical predictions.
AI.conclusion: The observed X-ray correlations confirm the corona's role in TDE emission. The corona's dynamics tie to jet activity, offering insights into both jetted and non-jetted TDEs. This method provides a framework for studying supermassive black hole activity via TDEs.

title: Supernova scores for active anomaly detection
summary: Large time-domain sky surveys generate extensive multi-year catalogs of light curves in which scientifically valuable transients, such as supernovae (SNe), are vastly outnumbered by artifacts and routine star variability. While supervised machine learning models can efficiently filter known classes, they struggle with extreme class imbalance and may overlook rare or novel events. Conversely, unsupervised anomaly detection provides broad discovery potential but lacks targeted sensitivity. We present a hybrid strategy that integrates a supervised SN probability score (SN-score) into the PineForest active anomaly detection framework to enhance SN discovery rate in the 23rd data release of the Zwicky Transient Facility. We train a binary classifier using light-curve features of spectroscopically confirmed SNe from the ZTF Bright Transient Survey, achieving a ROC-AUC approximately 0.98. Incorporating the SN-score as an additional feature, together with a small set of labeled priors, significantly accelerates the discovery of SN-like transients across ten extragalactic ZTF fields. This method increases discovery efficiency without compromising the ability to identify diverse astrophysical anomalies. Application of the combined methodology resulted in the discovery of seven previously unreported SN candidates, one AGN candidate, one unusual Galactic variable star SNAD283, as well as two host galaxies exhibiting multiple supernova events. These results demonstrate its value for scalable and expert-guided transient search in current and future surveys, including the Vera C. Rubin Observatory Legacy Survey of Space and Time.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a hybrid method combining supervised learning (SN-score) with unsupervised anomaly detection (PineForest) to improve supernova discovery efficiency in ZTF data, achieving high ROC-AUC and discovering new cosmic events.
AI.motivation: Supervised methods alone struggle with class imbalance and novel/rare events, while unsupervised methods lack specificity. The hybrid approach merges their strengths to enhance both precision and discovery potential in large sky survey datasets.
AI.method: Trained a binary SN classifier using confirmed SN light-curve features (ROC-AUC 0.98), then integrated the resulting SN-score into PineForest's anomaly detection framework. Added labeled priors to accelerate discovery of SN-like transients across 10 ZTF fields.
AI.result: Discovered 7 new SN candidates, 1 AGN candidate, 1 unusual variable star (SNAD283), and 2 host galaxies with multiple SNe. Maintained anomaly detection capabilities while improving SN discovery efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid method effectively balances targeted detection and broad anomaly discovery, offering a scalable solution for current/future surveys like Rubin Observatory's LSST.

title: Shadows of quintessence black holes: spherical accretion, photon trajectories, and geodesic observers
comment: 15 pages, 11 figures, 1 table. Accepted for publication in THE EUROPEAN PHYSICAL JOURNAL C
summary: The presence of a quintessence-like field can influence the black hole shadow through three primary mechanisms: the dynamics of accretion flows, the trajectories of photons, and the motion of observers. Unlike standard shadow analyses that assume a static observer at spatial infinity, the non-asymptotically flat nature of quintessence-corrected spacetimes motivates the consideration of freely falling (geodesic) observers. Using a perturbative approach, we derive analytical expressions for the event-horizon location, photon-sphere radius, innermost stable circular orbit, and critical impact parameter. We compute the observed intensity profiles for both static and infalling spherical accretion flows. We find that, although the photon-sphere radius and the critical impact parameter are invariant properties of the spacetime, the apparent angular size of the shadow depends sensitively on the observer's motion and location. Freely infalling observers systematically measure smaller angular radii than static observers at the same radius, whereas freely outgoing observers measure larger ones, in agreement with relativistic aberration. In contrast to the Schwarzschild case, the impact parameter alone is insufficient to characterize the observed angular structure in non-asymptotically flat spacetimes. Applying our results to the Event Horizon Telescope observation of M87$^\ast$, we show that more negative equations of state lead to stronger constraints on the quintessence parameter, largely independent of the observer prescription. Our analysis highlights the importance of carefully specifying the observer in shadow studies of non-asymptotically flat black-hole spacetimes.

AI.tldr: The paper explores how a quintessence-like field affects black hole shadows via accretion dynamics, photon paths, and observer motion, showing that observer's state (static vs. moving) significantly alters shadow observations.
AI.motivation: To address the non-asymptotically flat nature of spacetimes with quintessence fields, which necessitates considering geodesic observers instead of traditionally used static ones at infinity.
AI.method: Used a perturbative approach to derive analytical expressions for event-horizon, photon-sphere,ISCO, and critical impact parameter. Calculated intensity profiles for static and infalling accretion flows.
AI.result: Found that angular shadow size depends on observer motion/location; infalling observers measure smaller radii than static ones. Quintessence parameters constrain equations of state, independent of observer model.
AI.conclusion: Observer specification is critical in analyzing non-asymptotically flat black hole shadows; quintessence studies require careful observer consideration to avoid misinterpretation.

title: On the Robustness of type-II Seesaw Collider Searches
summary: Electroweak triplet Higgs sector extensions are well-motivated scenarios to address lepton flavour observations. These models can also be strongly constrained by combining precise, indirect low-energy measurements with direct searches for exotic, doubly charged Higgs bosons. Together, these searches set competitive constraints on the type-II seesaw mechanism. In this work, we consider extensions of the type-II seesaw, specifically through the lens of a modified collider phenomenology. Surveying motivated extensions, we map out changes in expected correlations, focusing on the modified production and decay phenomenology of exotic Higgs particles. This enables us to assess the robustness of the type-II seesaw collider constraints against extended new-physics contributions that modify standard sensitivity expectations and projections.

AI.tldr: The paper explores extended type-II seesaw models with focus on collider phenomenology of exotic Higgs particles, assessing how extended new-physics contributions impact collider constraints.
AI.motivation: Address lepton flavour anomalies via electroweak triplet Higgs extensions and evaluate combined constraints from low-energy measurements and collider searches on type-II seesaw mechanisms.
AI.method: Analyzing modified production/decay phenomenology of doubly charged Higgs bosons in extended type-II seesaw models, surveying extended scenarios to map correlations and test robustness of existing collider constraints.
AI.result: Shows how new-physics extensions can alter expected Higgs signatures, revealing limitations of current collider constraint analyses and pathways for future searches.
AI.conclusion: Collider constraints on type-II seesaw models are sensitive to beyond-standard contributions, necessitating improved search strategies that account for extended sectors in future experimental setups.

title: CubeSats Reach the Millisecond X-Ray Domain: Crab Pulsar Timing with SpIRIT/HERMES
comment: Accepted for publication in ApJ (9 March 2026)
summary: The High Energy Rapid Modular Ensemble of Satellites (HERMES) instrument is a compact X/$γ$-ray spectrometer operating on board the 6U (11 kg) SpIRIT CubeSat. The payload is particularly well suited for the observation of cosmic transients such as Gamma-Ray Bursts and bright pulsars thanks to its unique broadband sensitivity from a few keV to a few MeV and the temporal resolution down to half a microsecond. We report here the detection of the $\sim$33~ms Crab pulsar double-peaked pulse profile obtained by considering the canonical Crab ephemerides as provided by the Jodrell Bank catalog. We collected approximately 5.7$\cdot$10$^4$ photons from 730~s of observations, in the 3 keV -- 2 MeV energy band, during a single operation, and achieved a 5$σ$ pulse profile significance in the 3--11.5 keV energy band with binning at the ms scale. The results demonstrate that SpIRIT/HERMES can achieve millisecond timing accuracy at high energies and, thanks to its wide field of view and broad energy band, has the potential to contribute to GRB monitoring in the near future. Such capabilities were previously the domain of flagship observatories, underscoring the performance of the HERMES instrument with its compact form factor.

AI.tldr: The HERMES instrument on the SpIRIT CubeSat successfully detected the Crab pulsar's pulse profile, demonstrating millisecond timing accuracy in the 3-11.5 keV energy range. It leverages its compact design and broadband sensitivity for future GRB monitoring, rivaling larger observatories.
AI.motivation: To showcase the capabilities of the HERMES spectrometer in achieving high-energy, high-time-resolution observations from a small CubeSat platform, enabling future contributions to transient astronomy without needing flagship observatory scales.
AI.method: Collected ~57,000 photons over 730 seconds using HERMES' broadband sensitivity (3 keV-2 MeV) and microsecond temporal resolution. Used canonical Crab ephemerides for timing analysis, creating a binned pulse profile at millisecond scale.
AI.result: Observed the Crab pulsar's double-peaked profile with 5σ significance in the 3-11.5 keV band. Demonstrated millisecond timing precision, validating HERMES' potential for GRB monitoring.
AI.conclusion: HERMES' performance proves CubeSat-based instruments can achieve capabilities previously exclusive to large missions, opening new avenues for cost-effective, high-resolution astrophysical observations.

title: Data reduction method for OPTICAM multiband time series of transiting exoplanets
comment: 13 pages, 15 figures. Accepted for publication in RASTI
summary: We present a methodology for acquiring and reducing transiting exoplanet light curves obtained with the OPTICAM instrument in the Observatorio Astronómico Nacional en la Sierra de San Pedro Mártir (OAN-SPM). The OPTICAM sCMOS detectors generate significant warm pixels at exposures $\geq$10s, affecting both science and calibration frames. These warm pixels are not removed by standard dark subtraction because they vary unpredictably from frame to frame. We evaluate six pre-processing methods applied to science and calibration images using the transit of TOI-7149~b observed in g$^\prime$r$^\prime$i$^\prime$. A median filter with a 3$\times$3-pixel window minimizes the effect of warm pixels without affecting stellar signals. This median filter best reduces dispersion and red noise in the light curves when stellar peak counts are close to the dark current level. The improvement is less significant when the stellar peak is several thousand counts above the dark current level. We fit a multiband transit model to the light curves, measuring photometric precision, correlated noise, and retrieved planetary parameters. The transit model fitted to the light curves with pre-processing using a 3$\times$3-median filter achieves the highest Bayesian evidence. Thus, it is our recommended method for correcting warm pixels. Finally, we present a reduction pipeline that combines Python modules (PROFE) and AstroImageJ to implement our proposed method for OAN-SPM 2.1m+OPTICAM transiting planet observations.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a methodology to process OPTICAM light curves using a 3x3 median filter to mitigate warm pixel noise, particularly effective when stellar signals are near dark current levels. A custom pipeline combining PROFE and AstroImageJ is recommended for OAN-SPM exoplanet observations.
AI.motivation: To address warm pixel contamination in OPTICAM images caused by long exposures (>10s), which standard dark subtraction cannot handle effectively, impacting both science and calibration data.
AI.method: Six preprocessing methods were evaluated on multi-band transit data of TOI-7149 b. A 3x3 median filter was found most effective in reducing warm pixel noise without affecting stellar signals. They applied a multiband transit model to processed light curves.
AI.result: The median filtered method achieved highest Bayesian evidence, indicating better noise reduction and parameter retrieval. Improvement was most significant when stellar counts were near dark current levels, less so at higher stellar counts.
AI.conclusion: Recommend 3x3 median filtering as optimal preprocessing step for OPTICAM data. A new pipeline integrating PROFE and AstroImageJ is proposed to implement this for future transiting exoplanet observations with OAN-SPM.

title: Quasinormal modes and greybody factors of magnetically charged de Sitter black holes probed by massless external fields in Einstein Euler Heisenberg gravity
summary: This paper investigates the perturbation dynamics of massless scalar and electromagnetic fields on magnetically charged de Sitter (dS) black holes within the framework of string-inspired Euler-Heisenberg (EH) gravity. We calculate the quasinormal frequencies (QNFs) and discuss the influences of black hole magnetic charge $Q_{\mathrm{m}}$, the cosmological constant $Λ$, coupling parameter $ε$ and multipole number $l$ on QNFs, emphasizing the relationships between these parameters and quasinormal modes (QNMs) behavior. We find that the results obtained through the asymptotic iteration method (AIM) are in good agreement with those obtained by the WKB method. Importantly, the Bernstein spectral method is employed as a rigorous cross-check for QNFs in the $l=0$ scalar perturbation sector, where the WKB approximation is often unreliable. The greybody factor (GFs) is calculated using WKB method. The effects of the parameters $Q_{\mathrm{m}}$ and $ε$ on the greybody factor are also studied.

AI.tldr: This paper explores perturbation dynamics of massless scalar and electromagnetic fields on magnetically charged de Sitter black holes in Euler-Heisenberg gravity. It examines how magnetic charge, cosmological constant, coupling parameter, and multipole number affect quasinormal frequencies and modes, validates results via multiple methods (AIM, WKB, Bernstein), and analyzes greybody factors influenced by these parameters.
AI.motivation: To understand the stability and properties of magnetically charged de Sitter black holes under scalar/electromagnetic perturbations in modified gravity theories, especially considering the effects of string-inspired Euler-Heisenberg terms.
AI.method: The study uses the asymptotic iteration method (AIM), WKB approximation, and Bernstein spectral method to calculate quasinormal frequencies. Greybody factors are computed via WKB, with parameters Qm, Λ, ε, and l systematically varied to analyze their impacts.
AI.result: Quasinormal frequencies show sensitivity to magnetic charge, cosmological constant, coupling parameter, and multipole number. AIM and WKB results align well; Bernstein method confirms validity for l=0. Greybody factors reveal parameter-dependent modifications due to Euler-Heisenberg effects.
AI.conclusion: Magnetic charge and coupling parameters significantly influence black hole perturbations and radiation properties in Euler-Heisenberg gravity. The consistent results across methods validate the approach, supporting this framework's applicability to study modified gravity effects in cosmological black hole systems.

title: Exotic hadrons associated with $b$-quark
comment: 85 pages, 36 figures. Invited review for Physics Reports. Comments or corrections are welcome
summary: Compared to charmonium-like states, exotic hadrons associated with $b$-quark offer distinct advantages for exploring the nature of multiquark phenomena and the dynamics of the strong interaction. Due to the heavier bottom quark mass, theoretical calculations, particularly those based on effective field theories and potential models, tend to be more reliable and under better control in the bottomonium sector. With its clean $e^+e^-$ collision environment and high luminosity, the Belle and Belle II experiments are ideally suited to explore these exotic hadrons associated with $b$-quark, including $Z_b$, $X_b$, and $Y_b$ states, and charmonium-like states in $B$ decays. Utilizing the large proton--proton collision dataset, the LHCb experiment has conducted extensive investigations of heavy-flavor multiquark states through $B$ and $Λ_b$ decay channels. The relevant phenomenological interpretations are also reviewed.

AI.tldr: The paper highlights the advantages of studying bottomonium-based exotic hadrons, such as $Z_b$, $X_b$, $Y_b$, and charmonium-like states in $B$ decays, due to the heavier $b$ quark's theoretical predictability. It discusses experimental contributions from Belle/Belle II and LHCb, emphasizing their roles in advancing multiquark dynamics research.
AI.motivation: To leverage the strengths of bottom quark systems for more reliable theoretical analysis and experimental exploration of exotic hadrons, capitalizing on the cleaner environments and larger datasets from Belle II and LHCb.
AI.method: Analyzes existing experimental data from Belle/Belle II (using $e^+e^-$ collisions) and LHCb (proton-proton collisions) on $Z_b$, $X_b$, $Y_b$ states, and charmonium-like particles in $B/Λ_b$ decays. Reviews phenomenological models to interpret observations.
AI.result: Demonstrates how heavy flavor experiments provide key insights into multiquark structures and strong interaction dynamics, showing the viability of current experimental setups for discovering/explaining exotic hadron states.
AI.conclusion: Future studies using these experiments will deepen understanding of exotic hadron nature, with continued focus on precision measurements and improved theoretical modeling to clarify multiquark dynamics.

title: The period clustering of magnetars and X-ray dim isolated neutron stars
comment: Accepted for publication in Physics and Astronomy Reports
summary: The spin periods of magnetars and X-ray dim isolated neutron stars (XDINS) cluster within a remarkably narrow range. Using the current sample of 30 magnetars with measured periods (ranging from 0.33 to 11.78 s) and 8 XDINS (ranging from 3.45 to 12.76 s), we utilize the point-likelihood technique to constrain the birth and final periods of these sources, assuming a steady-state population. Employing a general braking law characterized by a constant braking index $n$, we find that for $n > 2$ the final (cut-off) period of magnetars is constrained to $P_f \simeq 11.8 - 12.0$ s and XDINS to $P_f \simeq 12.8 - 14.9$ s, at the 95 per cent confidence level, while the birth periods remains largely unconstrained for dipole spin-down ($n=3$) as in earlier work. The slight increase in the upper cutoff from $\sim$12 to $\sim$15 s over two decades of discoveries of new sources, yielding a threefold increase in the known magnetar population, and the extension of the minimum period to $\sim 0.33$ s strongly support a physical origin for this clustering. We discuss this result in the context of magnetic-field-decay models and fallback-disc torque-equilibrium scenarios. The combined magnetar and XDINS sample (38 sources) yields the tightest constraints on $P_f\simeq 12.8-12.9$ s, for $n=3$, suggesting possible evolutionary connections between these populations and pointing toward a common physical mechanism that terminates the observable phase of these neutron stars at periods near 14 s.

AI.tldr: The study analyzes the spin periods of magnetars and XDINS, finding their final periods are constrained near 12-15 seconds, suggesting a common physical mechanism stops their spin-down. Birth periods remain uncertain for dipole models but evolutionary links between these neutron star types are proposed.
AI.motivation: To understand why magnetars and XDINS have their spin periods clustered in a narrow range and to constrain their birth and final periods using observed data.
AI.method: .Applied the point-likelihood technique to a sample of 38 neutron stars (30 magnetars and 8 XDINS), assuming a steady-state population and a braking law with constant braking index n. Calculated constraints on final periods (Pf) at 95% confidence for different n values.
AI.result: For n>2, magnetars have Pf ~11.8-12.0s, XDINS ~12.8-14.9s. Combined samples suggest Pf ~12.8-12.9s for n=3. Birth periods unconstrained for n=3. The observed period clustering and evolution support magnetic field decay or fallback disc torque models.
AI.conclusion: The clustering implies a physical termination mechanism near ~14s. Possible evolutionary connections between magnetars and XDINS suggest shared processes in neutron star evolution, possibly involving magnetic field decay or disc torques at the end of their observable spin-down phases.

title: Deep Learning Search for Gravitational Waves from Compact Binary Coalescence
summary: Gravitational wave searches rely on a combination of methods, including matched filtering, coherent analyses, and more recent machine learning based pipelines. For compact binary coalescences, where signals originate from the relativistic dynamics of compact objects, matched filtering remains a central element, but its computational cost will increase substantially with the data volumes and parameter-space coverage required by next-generation interferometers such as the Einstein Telescope. Developing complementary strategies that reduce computational load while preserving detection performance is therefore essential. We investigate a hybrid approach that combines matched-filtering concepts with Convolutional Neural Networks, enabling efficient signal searches without relying on the usual $χ^2$ rejection test. Using simulated data sets that include injected signals in Gaussian noise, transient noise, and physical effects not represented in template bank, such as eccentricity, precession and higher-order modes, we show that the method achieves a detection efficiency comparable to a standard matched-filtering search while offering a more resource efficient pipeline. These results indicate that deep learning assisted searches can support sustainable gravitational-wave data analysis in future detector eras.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a hybrid method combining matched filtering with CNNs to reduce computational costs for gravitational wave detection in next-gen interferometers, maintaining detection performance.
AI.motivation: To address the increasing computational demands of gravitational wave searches for future detectors like the Einstein Telescope, which require handling larger data volumes and broader parameter spaces without sacrificing detection efficiency.
AI.method: A hybrid approach integrating matched-filtering and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) eliminates the need for the traditional χ² rejection test, using simulated datasets with injected signals in various noise conditions and physical effects.
AI.result: The method achieves detection efficiency comparable to standard matched-filtering but with better computational efficiency, suitable for sustainable data analysis in next-gen detector eras.
AI.conclusion: Deep learning-augmented searches are viable for reducing resource usage while maintaining performance, crucial for upcoming gravitational wave observatories.

title: Production of muonic kaon atoms at high-energy colliders
summary: We develop a framework for the formation of exotic muonic kaon atoms ($μK$) in semileptonic $D^{0}$ decays, using the effective weak Hamiltonian, a helicity-based treatment of the leptonic current, and a nonrelativistic bound-state projection. The resulting branching ratio, $\mathrm{BR}(D^{0}\!\to(μK)ν_μ)=2.29\times10^{-10}$, is implemented in a ROOT-based code to estimate yields at RHIC, LHC, and STCF. We show quantitatively that $μK$ atoms -- also produced through coalescence in the quark-gluon plasma (QGP) -- provide a sensitive probe of low-momentum primordial muons and early-time electromagnetic radiation, offering complementary constraints in an otherwise unexplored phase space for thermal dilepton and photon emission. Newly estimated dissociation cross sections in detector material indicate that secondary-vertex reconstruction should be experimentally feasible, allowing clean experimental identification of the atoms. Projected yields from QGP coalescence in LHC and RHIC heavy-ion collisions, and from $D^{0}$ decays in LHC high-luminosity $p+p$ collisions indicate that the first observation of $μK$ atoms is within reach.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a framework to study exotic muonic kaon atoms (μK) formed in semileptonic D⁰ decays, calculating their branching ratio and demonstrating their potential as probes of primordial muons and electromagnetic radiation in the quark-gluon plasma. It also evaluates experimental feasibility for detection at RHIC, LHC, and STCF.
AI.motivation: To explore a new method to probe low-momentum primordial muons and early-time electromagnetic radiation in the quark-gluon plasma, which are currently unobservable through existing thermal dilepton and photon emission studies. Additionally, to assess the experimental feasibility of detecting μK atoms through secondary-vertex reconstruction.
AI.method: Using the effective weak Hamiltonian, helicity-based leptonic current treatment, and nonrelativistic bound-state projection to model μK atom formation in D⁰ decays. Calculated branching ratio BR(D⁰→(μK)ν_μ)=2.29×10⁻¹⁰. Implemented in ROOT-based code to estimate yields at colliders. Compared contributions from both D⁰ decays and QGP coalescence, and computed dissociation cross sections in detector materials.
AI.result: The framework predicts observable μK atom yields in LHC and RHIC collisions. Dissociation cross section analysis confirms experimental feasibility via secondary-vertex detection. The study establishes μK atoms as a viable probe for unexplored thermal radiation signatures.
AI.conclusion: μK atoms offer a new experimental avenue to study the early QGP and test models of thermal electromagnetic radiation. The predicted yields and detection methods suggest their first observation is feasible in upcoming high-luminosity collider experiments.

title: Fast X-ray Transients produced by Off-axis Jet-Cocoons from Long Gamma-Ray Bursts
summary: Fast X-ray transients (FXTs) have been detected for over a decade, yet their origins are still enigmatic. The observed association between FXTs and broad-lined Type Ic supernovae (SNe Ic-BL) suggests that some may share the same progenitor with Long Gamma-Ray Bursts. In this work, we numerically simulate the long-term evolution of a relativistic jet propagating from inside the progenitor star up to the photon diffusion radius of the cocoon. Then we post-process the hydrodynamic results and calculate the cocoon cooling emission for various viewing angles from the jet axis. We find that, for viewing angles $θ_{\rm v}=10^{\circ}$--$20^{\circ}$, the off-axis cocoon emission can produce FXTs with luminosity $L_{\rm X}\simeq 10^{47-48} {\rm\, erg\,s^{-1}}$ and duration $t_{\rm X}\simeq 10$-$100\,$s. The observed spectra are quasi-thermal with the peak energy $E_{\rm peak}\simeq0.8$ keV. These properties naturally explain FXTs' observational features, including their high luminosity, soft spectra, and lack of gamma-ray counterparts. The Rayleigh-Jeans tail of the FXT spectra extends to the UV, producing an early UV flash simultaneously. As the cocoon expands and cools, the emission peak shifts to UV and optical bands, resulting in a bright optical plateau lasting for $\sim1$ day with color temperature $T_{\rm UV/opt} \simeq (1{-}3)\times10^{4}\,$K, before the emergence of supernova emission. Although our model underpredicts the UV/optical luminosity at $\sim1$ day, it still provides useful diagnostics for identifying the origins of FXTs.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes that fast X-ray transients (FXTs) originate from off-axis relativistic jets interacting with their progenitor's circumstellar medium, producing observable UV/optical signatures alongside X-ray emissions.
AI.motivation: The origin of FXTs remains unknown, but their association with SNe Ic-BL hints at a connection with relativistic jets like those in long Gamma-Ray Bursts. The authors aim to explain FXT observational features through jet-cocoon dynamics.
AI.method: Numerical simulations of relativistic jet evolution inside progenitor stars up to cocoon photon diffusion radii, followed by post-processing to compute off-axis cocoon emission spectra for viewing angles between 10°-20°.
AI.result: Cocoon cooling emissions at 10°-20° viewing angles reproduce key FXT properties: X-ray luminosities of ~10^47-48 erg/s, durations of 10-100 seconds, soft quasi-thermal spectra (peak ~0.8 keV), quasi-simultaneous UV flashes, and optical plateaus lasting ~1 day.
AI.conclusion: FXTs likely arise from off-axis relativistic jet breakout events, with their lack of gamma-ray counterparts explained by being viewed away from the jet axis; UV/optical diagnostics can now better identify FXT origins despite some luminosity discrepancies.

title: Relativistic Corrections to the Formation Rate of Extreme Mass-Ratio Inspirals
summary: Extreme mass-ratio inspirals (EMRIs) are long-duration gravitational-wave sources in which a compact object gradually spirals into a massive black hole. Their formation is governed by the interplay between stochastic angular-momentum diffusion driven by two-body relaxation and the dissipative evolution caused by gravitational-wave emission, with the loss-cone boundary deciding whether an object undergoes an inspiral or a direct plunge. Building on this physical picture, we construct a relativistically self-consistent analytic framework for estimating EMRI event rates. In Schwarzschild spacetime, we generalize the standard loss-cone angular momentum to an energy-dependent quantity and revise the plunge pericenter by using the minimum stable radius derived from general relativity. Relative to the Newtonian treatment, we show that incorporating these relativistic effects increases the predicted EMRI rates by roughly a factor of 8. This enhancement becomes more pronounced for shallower stellar density profiles and is insensitive to the mass of the central massive black hole, which emphasizes that relativistic effects are essential for EMRI rate estimations that are relevant for space-based gravitational-wave detectors, such as LISA and Taiji.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a relativistic framework for estimating extreme mass-ratio inspiral (EMRI) rates, showing that relativistic effects increase event rates by ~8x compared to Newtonian predictions, with implications for space-based detectors like LISA and Taiji.
AI.motivation: To accurately predict EMRI event rates for space-based gravitational-wave detectors, accounting for relativistic effects missed in Newtonian models.
AI.method: Generalizes loss-cone angular momentum to an energy-dependent quantity and uses general relativity's minimum stable radius to revise the plunge pericenter in Schwarzschild spacetime.
AI.result: Relativistic corrections increase EMRI rates by a factor of 8, especially for shallower density profiles, independent of central black hole mass.
