AI.conclusion: Relativistic effects are critical for accurate EMRI rate estimations, necessitating their inclusion in models for future gravitational-wave observations.

title: Extracting freeze-out conditions in beam energy scan via functional QCD
summary: Fluctuations of conserved charges provide a link between high quality theoretical results and precision measurements of heavy ion collisions. We compare results for ratios of the lowest order baryon number susceptibilities from functional QCD approaches to proton number cumulants extracted from experiments. We find that they meet at a specific temperature and chemical potential for each collision energy. This is indicative of the respective freeze-out point. From this self-consistent determination of the freeze-out parameters we extract a prediction for the kurtosis on the freeze-out line. We find quantitative agreement with experimental data where available, despite comparing apples (baryons) with oranges (protons). At a collision energy around 5 GeV, our kurtosis exhibits a peak structure indicative of the critical end point of QCD.

AI.tldr: The paper links theoretical calculations of baryon number susceptibilities from functional QCD approaches with experimental proton number cumulants from heavy ion collisions. It identifies freeze-out parameters where theoretical and experimental results align, predicts kurtosis values showing agreement with data, and suggests a critical end point near 5 GeV collision energy.
AI.motivation: To establish a connection between high-precision theoretical models of QCD and experimental measurements of heavy ion collisions, specifically to determine freeze-out conditions and locate the QCD critical end point.
AI.method: Comparison of baryon number susceptibilities from functional QCD methods with proton cumulant data from experiments across different collision energies. Analysis of freeze-out parameters where theory and experiment converge, and prediction of kurtosis behavior along the freeze-out line.
AI.result: Found agreement between theoretical and experimental results at specific freeze-out points for each collision energy. Predicted kurtosis peaks near 5 GeV, indicating a possible QCD critical end point, despite discrepancies between baryon and proton measurements.
AI.conclusion: The approach successfully links theoretical frameworks with experimental data to pinpoint freeze-out conditions and locate the critical end point, offering insights into QCD phase transitions even when comparing dissimilar observables (baryons vs. protons).

title: Joint Bayesian Source and Lens Reconstruction for Multi-messenger Binary Black Holes
comment: 6 pages, 1 figure. Submitted to the proceedings of MaxEnt 2025
summary: If a gravitational wave event is lensed by a cluster or galaxy in our line-of-sight, it is expected that its host galaxy would also be lensed. Therefore, connecting lensed gravitational wave events even without direct optical counterpart could be feasible by identifying matching lenses in electromagnetic data and surveys. Seminal work has demonstrated the potential of this approach in LVK, Euclid, HST, JWST, and CSST mock data, motivating the need for a dedicated software package to perform such analyses in practice. Here, we present the alpha-version of silmarel, the first software package designed to bridge these cosmic signals and enable us analysis of real LVK gravitational-wave binaries together with telescope observations from instruments like \textit{Euclid} or \textit{Hubble} Space Telescope, and the future of multimessenger binary black hole lensing.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces silmarel, a software package to analyze lensed gravitational wave events by correlating them with electromagnetic data from telescopes like Euclid and Hubble, enabling multimessenger observations of binary black hole systems.
AI.motivation: Lensed gravitational wave events are expected to have lensed host galaxies, allowing their identification through matching lenses in electromagnetic data. Previous studies showed potential in combining mock data from LVK, Euclid, HST, etc., necessitating a dedicated tool for practical analysis.
AI.method: Developed the alpha version of silmarel, the first software designed to integrate real LVK gravitational-wave data with telescope observations (Euclid, HST, JWST, CSST) to enable analysis of lensed binary black hole systems.
AI.result: Silmarel provides a framework to connect gravitational wave events with electromagnetic counterparts even without direct optical identification, paving the way for future multimessenger studies.
AI.conclusion: Silmarel is a critical step towards realizing the potential of gravitational lensing in multimessenger astronomy, facilitating analyses of upcoming observational data from current and future telescopes.

title: Dynamics of quadratic f(R) cosmology with a perfect fluid: Jordan and Einstein frames
summary: We investigate the global dynamics of the field equations of (pure) quadratic theories of gravity which generalise Einstein's theory in spatially flat homogeneous and isotropic cosmological models with a perfect fluid. We introduce global and regular 3-dimensional dynamical systems' formulations, on both the Jordan frame and the conformally related Einstein frame. The analysis in the Jordan frame explores the monotonicity properties of the interior flow which, together with the characterisation of the orbit structure on the 2-dimensional invariant boundaries and the desingularisation of non-hyperbolic fixed points, provides a global description of the flow and its limit sets. In the Einstein frame, the analysis uses the skew-product structure of the Einstein state space and the characterisation of the orbit structure on the 2-dimensional invariant boundaries. Furthermore, by obtaining asymptotic expansions we identify the solutions that are global conformally mapped from the Jordan frame to the Einstein frame and those that are not.

AI.tldr: The paper examines the global dynamics of quadratic gravity theories in spatially flat cosmological models using dynamical systems analysis on both Jordan and Einstein frames, identifying differences in solutions between the frames.
AI.motivation: To understand the behavior of cosmological models in quadratic gravity theories beyond Einstein's theory by analyzing their global dynamics through dynamical systems methods.
AI.method: Utilizes 3D dynamical systems formulations in both Jordan and Einstein frames, analyzing monotonicity, invariant boundaries, desingularization of fixed points, and skew-product structures. Asymptotic expansions are used to compare frame solutions.
AI.result: Provides a complete description of the flow's limit sets and orbit structures in both frames, distinguishing solutions that can be conformally mapped between frames from those that cannot.
AI.conclusion: Quadratic gravity models exhibit distinct global dynamics depending on the frame chosen, with some solutions unique to one frame, highlighting frame dependence in cosmological solutions.

title: Scattering of $Λ_{c}Λ_{c}$ and $Λ_{c}\barΛ_{c}$ in chiral effective field theory
summary: We investigate the $S$-wave scatterings of $Λ_cΛ_c$ and $Λ_{c}\bar Λ_{c}$ systems within a unified chiral effective field theory framework up to next-to-leading order. The contact low-energy coupling constants are determined by fitting to the lattice QCD results for the $Λ_cΛ_c$ scattering phase shift at an unphysical pion mass. After extrapolating to the physical pion mass, we find a repulsive interaction in the $I(J^P)=0(0^{+})$ $Λ_cΛ_c$ channel, consistent with the lattice QCD simulation. On the $Λ_{c}\bar Λ_{c}$ side, using the fitted contact low-energy constants, we predict the phase shifts and potentials for $Λ_c \barΛ_c$ scattering in the $I(J^{PC})=0(0^{-+})$ and $0(1^{--})$ channels. Attractive interactions are found in both channels, each allowing for the formation of bound states. In particular, the attraction in the $0(1^{--})$ $Λ_c \barΛ_c$ channel is stronger. In addition, our analysis reveals that the spin-spin term caused by the two-pion exchange contributes significantly to the interactions, leading to a distinct mass splitting between the $0(0^{-+})$ and $0(1^{--})$ $Λ_c \barΛ_c$ channels.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates $S$-wave scatterings of $Λ_cΛ_c$ and $Λ_{c} ar Λ_{c}$ systems using chiral effective field theory, identifying repulsive interactions in $Λ_cΛ_c$ and attractive interactions in both $Λ_c arΛ_c$ channels with significant spin-spin contributions.
AI.motivation: To explore the nature of $Λ_c$ baryon interactions using lattice QCD data and predict bound states in $Λ_c arΛ_c$ systems through chiral EFT, validating theoretical frameworks and understanding mass splittings.
AI.method: A unified chiral effective field theory framework up to next-to-leading order was employed. Contact couplings were fitted to lattice QCD data for $Λ_cΛ_c$ scattering phase shifts at unphysical pion masses, then extrapolated to physical pion mass. Phase shifts and potentials for $Λ_c arΛ_c$ channels were predicted using these parameters.
AI.result: Repulsive interaction in $Λ_cΛ_c$ (I=0, 0⁺ channel) aligns with lattice QCD. Attractive interactions in both $Λ_c arΛ_c$ channels (0⁻⁺ and 1⁻⁻) with stronger attraction in 1⁻⁻ channel, enabling bound state formation. Spin-spin terms from two-pion exchange significantly split masses between the two $Λ_c arΛ_c$ channels.
AI.conclusion: Chiral EFT effectively describes $Λ_c$ baryon interactions, predicts possible bound states in $Λ_c arΛ_c$ systems, and highlights the role of two-pion exchange in mass splitting, providing insights for future lattice QCD studies and experimental searches.

title: Comprehensive neutrino light curves and spectra: from pre-supernova evolution to early supernova phase
comment: 36 pages, 26 figures, 1 table, submitted to ApJS
summary: We present the first systematic study of neutrino emissions from massive stars, continuously tracking the late evolutionary stages through the early core-collapse supernova phase. Using progenitor and supernova models, we analyze the neutrino luminosities and spectra for progenitors with initial masses of 10--40~$M_\odot$. Our systematic analysis reveals that the compactness parameter ($ξ_{2.5}$) and carbon-oxygen core mass ($M_{\text{CO}}$) exhibit strong correlations with neutrino emission. In the pre-supernova phase, the time-integrated number of neutrinos correlates with $ξ_{2.5}$ when integrated over the final day and with $M_{\text{CO}}$ for longer durations. For the early supernova phase ($<200$ ms post-bounce), the neutrino properties are relatively insensitive to the specific stellar evolution code used, allowing for a reliable extraction of physical correlations. We confirm that the neutrino emission features, including the electron neutrino burst properties and accretion-powered luminosity of other species, reflect the progenitor's compactness. An evaluation of the observational feasibility for a nearby progenitor using a False Alarm Rate approach suggests that these correlations can persist even under practical detection conditions. Such a joint analysis of both phases provides complementary constraints on the internal structure. All calculated time-series data will be made publicly available.

AI.tldr: This paper presents the first systematic study of neutrino emissions from massive stars during their late evolution and early supernova phases, identifying correlations between neutrino properties and progenitor parameters like compactness (ξ_{2.5}) and core mass (M_{CO}).
AI.motivation: The motivation is to systematically analyze neutrino emissions from massive stars' late stages to understand their connection with progenitor structure and to provide observational constraints for astrophysical models.
AI.method: The method involves using progenitor and supernova models with initial masses 10–40 M☉ to calculate neutrino luminosities and spectra. The study tracks two phases: the pre-supernova phase (final day) and early supernova phase (<200 ms post-bounce), analyzing correlations with ξ_{2.5} and M_{CO}.
AI.result: Key results include strong correlations between neutrino emissions and progenitor parameters: time-integrated neutrinos correlate with ξ_{2.5} over short durations and M_{CO} over longer periods. Early supernova neutrino properties are robust against stellar evolution code choices. These correlations remain observable under practical detection conditions.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion asserts that combined analysis of pre-supernova and early supernova neutrino emissions offers complementary structural constraints on progenitors, with data publicly shared to facilitate future studies.

title: Cosmological Spacetimes with Sign-Changing Spatial Curvature and Topological Transitions
summary: Observational evidence, together with practical computations and modeling, supports a Euclidean spatial sector in the current cosmological model based on the FLRW metric. This, however, would imply that the total amount of matter and energy immediately after the Big Bang must have been infinite, an implication that could only be avoided through a transition from a closed to an open universe, a process forbidden in standard FLRW models. In this article, we investigate the spacetimes resulting from promoting the spatial curvature $k$ in FLRW spacetimes to a time-dependent function, $k \to k(t)$, allowing it to change sign and thereby allowing changes in the topology of the constant-$t$ slices. Although previously dismissed due to a classical theorem by Geroch, such transitions are shown to be consistent with global hyperbolicity when the comoving time is distinct from a Cauchy time, as recent work by one of the authors demonstrates. We construct three distinct geometries exhibiting this behavior using different representations of constant-curvature spaces. We analyze their global properties and identify mild conditions under which they remain globally hyperbolic. Furthermore, we characterize their Killing vectors, proving a general result for spherically symmetric spacetimes and compare them with known geometries in the literature.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes promoting the spatial curvature k in FLRW models to a time-dependent function k(t) to resolve the issue of infinite matter-energy content at the Big Bang and to allow topology changes, which were previously thought impossible due to Geroch's theorem. The authors construct three geometries demonstrating this, ensuring global hyperbolicity under certain conditions and analyzing their symmetries.
AI.motivation: The standard FLRW model with fixed spatial curvature implies infinite matter-energy density at the Big Bang and prohibits topology changes. The motivation is to avoid these inconsistencies by allowing k(t) to vary and potentially sign, thus enabling topology transitions while maintaining global hyperbolicity.
AI.method: The authors extend FLRW spacetimes by making spatial curvature k(t) time-dependent, bypassing Geroch's theorem through different comoving and Cauchy times. They construct three geometries using constant-curvature space representations, analyze their global properties, Killing vectors, and compare with existing models.
AI.result: The paper shows that k(t) spacetimes can maintain global hyperbolicity under mild conditions and allow topology transitions. Specific geometries are constructed, their symmetries characterized via Killing vectors, and their consistency with known spacetimes is demonstrated.
AI.conclusion: Allowing time-varying spatial curvature resolves the infinite-energy problem and enables forbidden topology changes while preserving global hyperbolicity. The constructed geometries offer new insights into early universe dynamics and must satisfy specific conditions to remain physically valid.

title: Beyond QED: Electroweak and hadronic extensions of McMule
comment: 11 pages, 3 figures, contribution to the 17th International Symposium on Radiative Corrections: Applications of Quantum Field Theory to Phenomenology (RADCOR2025), 5-10 October 2025, Puri, India
summary: McMule is a Monte Carlo framework developed to advance the low-energy precision frontier by providing QED corrections to leptonic scattering and decay processes, currently up to next-to-next-to-leading order. Recent developments have extended its capabilities in two important directions: the systematic inclusion of electroweak effects at low energies within the low-energy effective field theory [arXiv:2507.17652], and the incorporation of pion form factors and the non-perturbative hadronic vacuum polarisation in loop amplitudes through a combination of OpenLoops and effective field theory techniques, referred to as disperon QED [arXiv:2512.10709]. I will provide an overview of McMule and discuss these recent extensions and their applications. In particular, I will illustrate the impact of the model used for non-perturbative $γ$-$Z$ mixing effects in the context of the MOLLER experiment and highlight the subtlety involved in consistently aligning OpenLoops with its effective field theory expansion in disperon QED.

AI.tldr: McMule is a Monte Carlo framework providing QED corrections to low-energy processes at next-to-next-to-leading order. Recent extensions include incorporating electroweak effects using effective field theory and integrating pion form factors with non-perturbative hadronic vacuum polarization through disperon QED, aiding precision physics in experiments like MOLLER.
AI.motivation: To advance low-energy precision physics by systematically including electroweak effects and non-perturbative contributions in scattering/decay processes, ensuring accurate predictions for experiments like MOLLER.
AI.method: McMule uses Monte Carlo methods for QED corrections. Recent updates added electroweak effects via low-energy EFT, and combined OpenLoops with EFT techniques (disperon QED) to handle pion form factors and hadronic vacuum polarization in loop amplitudes.
AI.result: The extensions allow precise modeling of γ-Z mixing in MOLLER and demonstrate challenges in aligning OpenLoops with EFT expansions, showcasing the framework's capability in high-precision experiments.
AI.conclusion: The developments in McMule strengthen its role in precision calculations by addressing electroweak and non-perturbative effects, though careful alignment between computational tools and EFT expansions remains crucial for accuracy.

title: Scattering from compact objects: Debye series and Regge-Debye poles
summary:
We investigate elastic scattering by a compact, horizonless body in curved spacetime, considering a massless scalar wave incident on a static, spherically symmetric, uniform-density star of radius $R$ and mass $M$ with a Schwarzschild exterior. We introduce an exact Debye-series decomposition of the scattering matrix, in the spirit of Debye expansions in Mie scattering. This decomposition separates direct surface reflection from contributions involving transmission into the interior and subsequent propagation, and admits a natural trajectory interpretation. We then determine the associated Regge-Debye pole spectrum in the complex angular-momentum plane. For neutron-star-like tenuities ($R>3M$), the spectrum exhibits two pole families: a surface-wave branch associated with the surface matching condition and a broad-resonance branch associated with the interior regularity condition. For ultracompact objects ($R<3M$), the surface-wave branch persists, while the interior-resonance sector splits into broad- and narrow-resonance branches.
  We next reconstruct the scattering amplitude from the Debye partial-wave contributions and find excellent agreement with direct partial-wave calculations. Finally, we develop complex angular-momentum representations order by order in the Debye series, making explicit how the pole families and non-pole sectors contribute to each Debye term. In the neutron-star-like regime, we find a genuine competition between Regge-Debye pole sums and branch-cut contributions, and show that, at high frequency, the rainbow-like enhancement already arises from the first interior-transmission contribution and is dominated by the interior-resonance Regge-Debye poles. By contrast, in the ultracompact regime, the Debye amplitudes are overwhelmingly pole dominated.

AI.tldr: The paper examines elastic scattering of massless scalar waves by a static, spherically symmetric star using an exact Debye-series decomposition. It identifies distinct pole families in different object regimes and shows how scattering amplitudes depend on poles and branch cuts.
AI.motivation: To understand scattering dynamics in curved spacetime involving horizonless compact objects, distinguishing surface vs interior contributions and analyzing Regge-Debye pole behavior across neutron-star-like (R>3M) and ultracompact (R<3M) regimes.
AI.method: Debye-series decomposition of the scattering matrix, Regge-Debye pole spectrum analysis in complex angular-momentum plane, comparison with direct partial-wave calculations, and iterative complex angular-momentum representations.
AI.result: Two distinct pole families observed in neutron-star-like objects (surface and broad resonances) and three in ultracompact ones (surface, broad, and narrow resonances). High-frequency rainbow enhancement arises early from interior poles; ultracompact regimes are pole-dominated.
AI.conclusion: The Debye-series approach successfully disentangles scattering contributions from surface reflection and internal transmission. The regime dependence of pole dominance and branch-cut interactions reveals fundamental differences in scattering physics between neutron-star and ultracompact objects.

title: An asymptotic proof of the classical log soft graviton theorem
summary: We present a derivation of the classical log soft graviton theorem within the asymptotic framework of Compère, Gralla, and Wei. The proof relies solely on Einstein equations near timelike, spatial, and null infinity, together with matching properties across these regions. The approach is fully covariant under time reversal and incorporates contributions from incoming soft radiation. In the absence of incoming memory one recovers the standard log soft factor, which features an asymmetry between future and past hard components. From an asymptotic perspective, the origin of this asymmetry lies in a long-known discontinuity of the gravitational field at spatial infinity.

AI.tldr: The paper derives the classical log soft graviton theorem using the asymptotic framework of Compère, Gralla, and Wei, establishing its dependence on Einstein equations near various infinities and the role of incoming soft radiation in asymmetry between future and past hard components.
AI.motivation: To understand the origin of the asymmetry in the log soft graviton theorem and clarify how incoming radiation and discontinuities at spatial infinity influence gravitational field behavior under asymptotic conditions.
AI.method: Utilizes the asymptotic framework of Compère, Gralla, and Wei, analyzing Einstein equations near timelike, spatial, and null infinities, and their matching properties. Considers contributions from incoming soft radiation and the effect of time reversal covariance.
AI.result: Demonstrates that the standard log soft factor's asymmetry arises from a discontinuity in the gravitational field at spatial infinity when no incoming memory is present. Confirms the theorem's derivation without assuming incoming radiation but shows asymmetry depends on its absence.
AI.conclusion: The asymmetry in the log soft graviton theorem stems from fundamental asymptotic properties of spacetime, particularly the discontinuity at spatial infinity, underscoring the importance of considering all boundary regions in gravitational dynamics.

title: Vector-like dark matter within an alternative left-right symmetric model
summary: We investigate an extension of the left-right symmetric model featuring an additional non-abelian $SU(2)$ gauge symmetry. The particle content is augmented by one generation of vector-like leptons transforming under the fundamental representation of this new gauge group. We demonstrate that the neutral component of the vector-like lepton multiplet naturally provides a viable and stable dark matter candidate. Stability is ensured by imposing a discrete parity symmetry that forbids mixing between the vector-like leptons and the Standard Model leptons. As a consequence, the dark sector interacts with the visible sector exclusively through the vector portal (via s-channel processes) and the vector-like lepton portal (via t-channel processes). In our analysis, we incorporate collider constraints on the mass of the first-generation extra charged gauge boson $W^{\prime\pm}$, while assuming that additional scalar states are decoupled from the relevant energy scale for simplicity. We identify the regions of parameter space consistent with the observed relic abundance, collider bounds on the charged partner $E^{\pm}$, current direct detection limits from the LZ experiment and indirect detection constraints from Fermi-LAT. We find viable dark matter with a mass at the TeV scale. We show the complementarity of direct and indirect searches in probing the remaining parameter space of the model, in particular comparing the prospects of multi-ton direct detection experiments such as XLZD and of the CTA telescope.

AI.tldr: The paper explores an extended left-right symmetric model with an additional SU(2) gauge symmetry and vector-like leptons, proposing a stable dark matter candidate protected by a discrete parity symmetry.
AI.motivation: To investigate dark matter stability and interactions within an extended gauge model, addressing Collider and direct/indirect detection constraints.
AI.method: Augment the model with vector-like leptons, enforce discrete parity symmetry for stability, and analyze parameter space using relic abundance constraints and experimental bounds.
AI.result: Viable TeV-scale dark matter is consistent with observed data; collider and detection experiments like XLZD and CTA can probe remaining parameter space.
AI.conclusion: The model successfully accommodates stable dark matter, with future experiments required to fully test its predictions.

title: Globally stable, ghost-free hyperbolic square-root deformation of the Starobinsky model
summary: We propose an exact, analytic deformation of the Starobinsky model governed by the strictly positive derivative of its Lagrangian, $f'(R) = αR + \sqrt{α^2 R^2 + 1}$, with $α> 0$. This geometric hyperbolic square-root ansatz is designed to eliminate the well-known strong-coupling singularity that arises in quadratic $f(R)$ gravity when $f'(R)=0$. The construction seamlessly recovers general relativity at low curvatures and preserves the successful slow-roll inflationary plateau at extreme positive curvatures. In the limit $R \to -\infty$, the derivative $f'(R)$ asymptotes to zero strictly from above, removing the pathological branch associated with the vanishing of $f'(R)$. This guarantees that the only admissible constant-curvature ($R=A$) solutions correspond to standard Einstein spaces with an effective cosmological constant $Λ_{\text{eff}} \equiv A/4$. The first and second derivatives of the action, as well as the scalaron mass squared, remain strictly positive globally, ensuring a perfectly ghost-free and tachyon-free cosmological evolution across the entire spacetime manifold. In the Einstein frame, the dynamics of the scalaron is governed by the globally defined potential $V(φ) = \frac{1}{8α} [ 1 - (1 + 2\sqrt{2/3}φ) \exp(-2\sqrt{2/3}φ) ] + Λ\exp(-2\sqrt{2/3}φ)$, which naturally establishes an impenetrable energetic wall as $φ\to -\infty$, offering a robust, globally stable mechanism for non-singular bouncing cosmologies. For $N = 60$ inflationary e-folds, the model predicts a scalar spectral index of $n_s \simeq 0.967$ and a strongly suppressed tensor-to-scalar ratio of $r \simeq 0.00083$, which position the proposed theory within the observationally favored parameter space of the Planck and BICEP/Keck Array baseline constraints.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a modified Starobinsky model using a hyperbolic square-root ansatz for the Lagrangian's derivative to eliminate strong-coupling singularities and enable ghost/tachyon-free cosmology, predicting observational parameters within Planck/BICEP/Keck limits.
AI.motivation: To address the strong-coupling singularity in quadratic f(R) gravity where f'(R)=0, and ensure a stable, singularity-free cosmological framework compatible with inflationary predictions.
AI.method: Proposes an analytic deformation of the Starobinsky model with f'(R) = αR + √(α²R² +1), avoiding f'(R)=0 at all curvatures. Analyzes the resulting Einstein frame potential and cosmological implications, ensuring positivity of action derivatives and scalaron mass.
AI.result: The model recovers GR at low curvatures, maintains inflationary success, eliminates pathological solutions for R→-infty, and produces an impenetrable energetic wall in the Einstein frame. Predicts n_s ~0.967 and r ~0.00083 for 60 e-folds.
AI.conclusion: The model provides a globally stable, non-singular bouncing cosmology with observational predictions matching Planck/BICEP constraints, offering a robust alternative to standard f(R) gravity approaches.

title: Joint Bayesian analysis of soft and high-$p_\perp$ probes yields tighter constraints on QGP properties
summary: To extract bulk QGP properties, we perform a joint Bayesian calibration of bulk-medium parameters using low-$\pt$ bulk and high-$\pt$ tomography within a common medium evolution. Low-$\pt$ observables are computed with \textsc{TRENTo}+\textsc{VISHNU}; temperature profiles are passed to \textsc{DREENA-A} to predict light/heavy $R_{\mathrm{AA}}(\pt)$ and $v_2(\pt)$. Gaussian-process emulation enables Hamiltonian Monte Carlo sampling of the low-$\pt$-only and joint posteriors. The low-$\pt$-only case underpredicts high-$\pt$ anisotropy; the joint calibration matches both sectors and markedly tightens bulk-parameter constraints, demonstrating the added power of high-$\pt$ data.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a joint Bayesian calibration method combining low- and high-pT data to improve constraints on bulk quark-gluon plasma (QGP) properties, using models TRENTo+VISHNU and DREENA-A, with Gaussian-process emulation for efficient sampling.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of using only low-pT observables in constraining bulk QGP parameters and to leverage high-pT tomography for more accurate and tighter parameter constraints.
AI.method: Joint Bayesian calibration of bulk-medium parameters using low-pT bulk observables (via TRENTo+VISHNU) and high-pT spectra (via DREENA-A). Gaussian-process emulation enables efficient Hamiltonian Monte Carlo sampling of combined posteriors.
AI.result: Low-pT-only analysis underpredicts high-pT anisotropy, while joint calibration matches both data sets and significantly tightens parameter constraints, highlighting the added value of high-pT data.
AI.conclusion: Integrating high-pT tomography with low-pT bulk observables in a unified framework provides superior accuracy and tighter constraints on QGP properties compared to separate analyses.

title: Strong deflection of massive particles via the geodesic deviation equation
summary: We develop a formulation of the strong deflection limit for the scattering of particles following timelike geodesics in asymptotically flat, static, and spherically symmetric spacetimes. For fixed specific energy, as the angular momentum approaches its critical value from above, the particle passes arbitrarily close to the associated unstable circular orbit, undergoes many windings around it, and the deflection angle diverges logarithmically. Using the geodesic deviation equation, we show covariantly that the coefficient of this logarithmic divergence is determined by the radial instability exponent of the critical trajectory, defined per unit azimuthal angle. We express this instability exponent in terms of local curvature data on the unstable circular orbit, thereby providing both kinematic and geometric interpretations of the strong deflection limit. In general relativity, its matter dependence enters only through a single local scalar combination constructed from the static-frame energy density and the principal radial and tangential pressures.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a covariant formulation for the strong deflection limit of particle scattering in asymptotically flat spacetimes, showing the deflection angle diverges logarithmically with angular momentum approaching a critical value. The divergence's coefficient relates to the orbit's instability exponent derived from local curvature data.
AI.motivation: To understand extreme scattering scenarios where particles approach unstable circular orbits, leading to large deflections and complex trajectories, requiring a general covariant framework beyond specific spacetime models.
AI.method: Uses geodesic deviation equations and analysis of unstable circular orbits' radial instability exponents. Expresses the instability exponent using local curvature invariants involving energy density and pressure components.
AI.result: Derives a universal expression linking the logarithmic divergence coefficient to spacetime curvature properties, showing matter effects enter only via specific scalar combinations of energy density and pressures.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a covariant geometric framework for strong deflection scattering applicable to any static spherically symmetric spacetime, emphasizing the role of local curvature in determining critical scattering behavior.

title: Circumstellar Medium of Supernovae as New Probes for Feebly-interacting Particles
summary: We propose a novel strategy to probe feebly-interacting particles (FIPs) by exploiting the dense, confined circumstellar medium (CSM) surrounding core-collapse supernovae (CCSNe). FIPs produced in the proto-neutron star can deposit substantial visible energy into the CSM via decay prior to the shock breakout from the progenitor star. This energy injection heats and ionizes the CSM, establishing a FIP-induced photosphere that generates distinctive precursor blackbody emission. Using early-time observations of SN 2023ixf, we translate the non-detection of excessive precursor luminosity into stringent new constraints on MeV-scale dark photons as an exemplary model. Our results significantly extend existing CCSN bounds and exclude previously unexplored regions of parameter space. We further demonstrate that the FIP-induced dust sublimation offers robust diagnostics for future Galactic SNe, opening a new avenue to explore the dark sector.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a method to detect feebly-interacting particles (FIPs) using the circumstellar medium around core-collapse supernovae, applying it to constrain MeV-scale dark photons through observations of SN 2023ixf and proposing future applications for dark sector exploration.
AI.motivation: To explore new ways of detecting FIPs and to constrain dark matter models, particularly MeV-scale dark photons, by leveraging the unique environment of CCSN events and their circumstellar medium.
AI.method: The method involves analyzing the interaction of FIPs with the dense CSM around CCSNe. FIPs from the proto-neutron star deposit energy into the CSM before shock breakout, creating a photosphere that emits precursor blackbody radiation. Observations of SN 2023ixf are used to derive constraints by comparing expected versus observed precursor luminosity. Simulations of dust sublimation caused by FIP energy deposition are also employed.
AI.result: Non-detection of excessive precursor luminosity in SN 2023ixf led to tighter constraints on MeV-scale dark photon parameters, excluding previously uncharted parameter spaces. The FIP-induced dust sublimation effect is identified as a reliable diagnostic tool for future supernova observations.
AI.conclusion: The study establishes a novel observational strategy for probing FIPs and dark sector particles via CCSN environments, extending previous bounds and offering a promising avenue for future dark matter research through early-time supernova observations.

title: Interaction of the gravitational Hawking radiation and a static point mass
summary: We study the interaction of a stress-energy tensor describing a static point mass supported by a string outside a Schwarzschild black hole with the gravitons of the Hawking radiation. We derive a closed-form analytic expression for the total response rate of this stress-energy tensor to the thermal gravitons in the Unruh state, which models the quantum state in the spacetime of a spherically symmetric black hole formed by gravitational collapse. This response rate is finite in contrast with the infrared divergent response rate for a static point mass supported by a string in Rindler spacetime, i.e., a point mass accelerated uniformly by a string in Minkowski spacetime. By comparing the response rate near the black hole horizon with that in Rindler spacetime, we show that the size of the black hole acts as a natural infrared cutoff. We also find that the response rate of this stress-energy tensor to the thermal gravitons incoming from past null infinity in the Hartle-Hawking state vanishes. As a result, the total response rate of a static point mass (supported by a string) in the Unruh and Hartle-Hawking states for gravitons are identical. This is also the case for a static charge interacting with the electromagnetic field but not for a static source for a massless scalar field.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes the interaction between a static point mass supported by a string outside a Schwarzschild black hole and Hawking radiation gravitons, finding a finite response rate in the Unruh state. This contrasts with the infrared divergent response in Rindler spacetime, with the black hole size acting as an infrared cutoff. The response to Hartle-Hawking gravitons from past null infinity is zero, making total response rates identical for both states, a behavior unique to gravitational and electromagnetic fields compared to scalar fields.
AI.motivation: To understand how quantum effects of black hole Hawking radiation interact with local stress-energy tensors, resolving divergences observed in flat spacetime (Rindler) scenarios and exploring the role of black hole horizon structure as a natural cutoff.
AI.method: Derived closed-form expressions for stress-energy response rates using Unruh and Hartle-Hawking states, compared black hole and Rindler spacetime scenarios, and analyzed boundary conditions for gravitons incoming from past null infinity.
AI.result: Finite response rates in Schwarzschild spacetime due to black hole's infrared cutoff; zero response to Hartle-Hawking gravitons from past null infinity; equivalence of total response rates between Unruh/Hartle-Hawking states for gravitational interactions, matching electromagnetic but differing from scalar field interactions.
AI.conclusion: Black hole geometry inherently regularizes quantum stress-energy interactions, differing from flat spacetime accelerations. The equivalence in response rates between thermal states suggests deep connections between black hole thermodynamics and quantum field boundaries, with distinctions across field types indicating field-specific coupling behaviors.

title: JLab and J-PARC for the J/{\ensuremathψ} Production at the Threshold
summary: New threshold measurements for $γ~p\to p~J/ψ\to p~(μ^+μ^-)$ by 007 and $γ^\ast~p\to p~J/ψ\to p~(e^+e^-)$ by CLAS12 allow us to extend the previous phenomenological determination of the J/\ensuremathψ-proton scattering length, $α_{J/ψp}$, using GlueX threshold data for $γ~p\to p~J/ψ\to p~(e^+e^-)$. The agreement between all three J/\ensuremathψ data sets shows no indication of systematic differences between methodologies. Furthermore, perturbative QCD predictions support the phenomenological determination of heavy vector meson-nucleon scattering lengths. Upcoming J-PARC threshold measurements of the reaction $π^-~p\to n~J/ψ\to n~(l^+l^-)$ will help resolve a puzzle regarding the trend of the vector meson-nucleon scattering lengths, $|α_{Υp}| \ll |α_{J/ψp}| \ll |α_{φp}| \ll |α_{ωp}|$, which is consistent with the ``young vector meson'' hypothesis.

AI.tldr: New threshold measurements from 007, CLAS12, and GlueX for γp→pJ/ψ processes support the phenomenological determination of J/ψ-proton scattering lengths, aligning with perturbative QCD predictions. Upcoming J-PARC π^-p→nJ/ψ experiments aim to resolve discrepancies in vector meson-nucleon scattering lengths trends, consistent with the 'young vector meson' hypothesis.
AI.motivation: To address the puzzle of the trend in vector meson-nucleon scattering lengths (e.g., why |α_{ϒp}| < |α_{J/ψp}| < |α_{φp}| < |α_{ωp}|) and verify consistency with the 'young vector meson' hypothesis through combined analysis of new and existing threshold data.
AI.method: Analysis of threshold measurements from 007 (γp→pJ/ψ→pμ+μ-), CLAS12 (γ* p→pJ/ψ→pe+e-), and GlueX (γp→pJ/ψ→pe+e-) to determine J/ψp scattering length. Comparisons between datasets and perturbative QCD predictions.
AI.result: All J/ψ datasets show agreement without systematic discrepancies, supporting the phenomenological scattering length values. The observed trend aligns with the 'young vector meson' hypothesis predicting decreasing scattering lengths for heavier mesons.
AI.conclusion: Upcoming J-PARC π^-p→nJ/ψ experiments will help resolve remaining questions about the inverse hierarchy in vector meson-nucleon scattering lengths, emphasizing the need for charm-strange meson data to validate the hypothesis fully.

title: The eikonal spin-dependent Odderon and gluon Sivers function of a proton, and its small-$x$ evolution
summary: The matrix element in the proton of the eikonal Odderon operator, with a helicity flip, has been shown to correspond to the dipole gluon Sivers function. We employ a three quark light-front model of the proton to determine the Sivers function at moderately small $x_0 \sim 0.1$ and transverse momentum $k_\perp \lesssim 1$~GeV. The model light-cone (LC) wave function predicts the properties of $x f_{1T}^{\perp g}(x,k_\perp)$ such as its overall magnitude, the position of its peak in $k_\perp$, and its behavior at small $k_\perp$. We then compute numerically the BFKL anomalous dimension characterizing the power-law tail at $k_\perp \gtrsim 1.5$~GeV of the gluon Sivers function at small LC momentum fractions, $α_s \log x_0/x = 1$: $x f_{1T}^{\perp g}(x,k_\perp) \sim k_\perp^{-3.3}$.

AI.tldr: The study explores the proton's dipole gluon Sivers function using a light-front quark model, predicting its behavior at moderate partonic fractions and transverse momenta, and calculates the BFKL anomalous dimension for its high-$k_\perp$ tail.
AI.motivation: To understand the helicity-flip matrix element of the eikonal Odderon operator in the proton, which corresponds to the dipole gluon Sivers function, and provide insights into its dependence on partonic momentum fractions and transverse momentum.
AI.method: Employing a three-quark light-front proton model to compute the gluon Sivers function, analyzing its properties such as magnitude, peak position in $k_\perp$, and low $k_\perp$ behavior. Numerically evaluating the BFKL anomalous dimension at $\alpha_s \log x_0/x = 1$.
AI.result: The function peaks at $k_\perp \lesssim 1$ GeV, showing a power-law tail $\sim k_\perp^{-3.3}$ for $k_\perp \gtrsim 1.5$ GeV. The model successfully describes features like peak position and magnitude.
AI.conclusion: The light-front model effectively captures the gluon Sivers function's characteristics, validating its utility for studying transverse momentum dependent functions and Odderon dynamics in hadrons.

title: Directed Flow of D and B Mesons in an Electrically and Chirally Conductive QGP at LHC Energies
summary: We investigate the directed flow of D and B mesons in the presence of electromagnetic fields incorporating finite electrical and chiral conductivities at LHC energies. The momentum evolution of heavy quarks in the quark-gluon plasma (QGP) is studied using Langevin dynamics, with their interactions with the medium described within the extended quasiparticle model (QPMp) framework. The electromagnetic fields are obtained from analytical solutions of Maxwell equations that account for both electrical and chiral conductivities. These conductivities modify the space-time evolution of the electromagnetic fields and influence the splitting of the directed flow between mesons and anti-mesons. However, the influence of chiral conductivity remains secondary to that of electrical conductivity and its impact on the directed flow is marginal. The results show that heavy mesons containing a charm quark develop a directed flow with a sign opposite to that of heavy mesons containing a bottom quark, with a smaller magnitude for the latter. The present study indicates that a simultaneous experimental measurement of v1 for heavy mesons containing both charm and bottom quarks would provide valuable insight into the electromagnetic field origin of v1 for heavy quarks.

AI.tldr: The study examines directed flow differences between D (charm) and B (bottom) mesons in quark-gluon plasma (QGP) under electromagnetic fields, using Langevin dynamics with finite electrical and chiral conductivities. It finds opposing flow directions with smaller magnitude for B mesons, suggesting combined experimental measurements could reveal electromagnetic field origins of heavy quark flow.
AI.motivation: To understand the role of electromagnetic fields and their conductivities in modifying heavy meson directed flow (v₁) patterns in high-energy collisions, and to explore distinctions between charm and bottom quark systems as probes of QGP properties.
AI.method: Langevin dynamics simulation incorporating extended quasiparticle model (QPMp) for heavy quark-medium interactions. Analytical Maxwell solutions include both electrical and chiral conductivities to model electromagnetic fields' evolution. Tracks meson-antimeson flow asymmetry due to field effects.
AI.result: Charm (D) and bottom (B) mesons exhibit directed flows with opposite signs and smaller magnitude for B mesons. Chiral conductivity's influence is minor compared to electrical conductivity. Opposite v₁ signs between charm and bottom systems are key findings.
AI.conclusion: Experimental measurement of both charm and bottom heavy meson v₁ could clarify electromagnetic origins of directed flow in QGP. The secondary role of chiral conductivity compared to electrical conductivity is highlighted, suggesting focus on the latter for flow modeling.

title: Dimuon production in neutrino-nucleus collisions at next-to-next-to-leading order in perturbative QCD
summary: Charm production in charged-current neutrino-nucleus deep-inelastic scattering (DIS), measured through dimuon final states, remains an important constraint of strangeness in global analyses of parton distribution functions (PDFs). This process has traditionally favored a smaller strange-quark PDF at small momentum fractions $x$ than what the LHC heavy-gauge boson data have indicated. Here, we present a self-contained next-to-next-to-leading-order (NNLO) perturbative QCD calculation of dimuon production in neutrino-nucleus DIS based on semi-inclusive DIS (SIDIS). This process has been previously computed at NNLO through fully inclusive charm production. We discuss the shortcomings of this approach and how they are addressed in the SIDIS picture. We study the perturbative convergence and explore new heavy-quark production channels that become available at NNLO. We find that the NNLO corrections significantly reduce the scale uncertainties at large values of $x$ where the cross sections are enhanced by the NNLO corrections. At small $x$, the NNLO corrections tend to be negative instead, which alleviate the tension between the dimuon and LHC data.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a NNLO perturbative QCD calculation of dimuon production in neutrino-nucleus DIS using the SIDIS approach, addressing previous inconsistencies between charm production data and LHC results by reducing scale uncertainties and alleviating tensions at small x.
AI.motivation: To resolve the discrepancy between neutrino DIS measurements of strange quark PDFs at small x and LHC data, which previously suggested smaller strange-quark PDF values. Existing calculations were limited, so a more accurate NNLO framework using SIDIS is developed.
AI.method: A NNLO perturbative QCD calculation of dimuon production in neutrino-nucleus DIS based on semi-inclusive DIS (SIDIS), addressing shortcomings of previous inclusive approaches. New heavy-quark production channels at NNLO and perturbative convergence are analyzed.
AI.result: NNLO corrections reduce scale uncertainties at large x where cross-sections increase, and at small x they lower cross-sections (negative corrections), reducing tension between dimuon and LHC datasets.
AI.conclusion: The improved NNLO/SIDIS framework provides better convergence and reduced uncertainties, offering a more consistent picture between neutrino DIS and LHC data regarding strange quark distributions.

title: Extracting the speed of sound of QCD from transverse momentum fluctuations
summary: We extract the speed of sound ($c_s$) in the quark-gluon plasma from ATLAS data on the probability distribution of the transverse momentum per particle, $[p_T]$, in ultra-central Pb+Pb collisions. With an ideal detector, $c_s$ can be inferred from the rise of the mean $[p_T]$ with the collision multiplicity. In practice, however, low-$p_T$ particles escape detection, which biases the analysis. We show how to correct for this bias by using data on the variance of $[p_T]$, as well as information from the recently-measured $v_0(p_T)$. We also introduce a systematic method for deblurring the noise from the hadronization process. Assuming that the size of the quark-gluon plasma is independent of the hadron multiplicity in collisions at zero impact parameter, which is the scenario preferred both by high-energy QCD and heavy-ion data, we obtain $c_s/c=0.496\pm 0.008$ at temperature $T=221\pm 13$ MeV, in perfect agreement with first-principles calculations from lattice QCD.

AI.tldr: The paper extracts the speed of sound in the quark-gluon plasma from ATLAS data using transverse momentum analysis, correcting for detector bias and hadronization noise, resulting in a value consistent with lattice QCD predictions.
AI.motivation: To accurately determine the speed of sound in the quark-gluon plasma by addressing detection biases caused by low-$p_T$ particle loss and hadronization effects, which are critical for validating theoretical models like lattice QCD.
AI.method: The method involves analyzing the mean and variance of transverse momentum per particle in Pb+Pb collisions, correcting for low-$p_T$ particle detection bias using variance data and $v_0(p_T)$ measurements. A systematic approach is applied to deblur noise from hadronization. The analysis assumes quark-gluon plasma size independence from hadron multiplicity at zero impact parameter.
AI.result: The extracted speed of sound is $c_s/c = 0.496 ± 0.008$ at $T = 221 ± 13$ MeV, aligning with lattice QCD calculations.
AI.conclusion: The results validate the theoretical consistency of lattice QCD predictions for the quark-gluon plasma properties and demonstrate effective correction methods for experimental biases in high-energy collision data.

title: Probing GPDs in exclusive electroproduction of dijets
summary: We summarize the formalism for calculating the exclusive dijet production in $e p \to e^{\prime} jj p$ in collinear QCD factorization, using generalized parton distributions (GPDs) as the soft hadronic input modeled in the double distribution (DD) approach. We include all leading-order contributions coming from light sea and valence quark exchanges, and gluon exchanges for both light quark-antiquark ($q\bar{q}$) production and also the heavy $c\bar{c}$ final state. We present results for several differential distributions for the cross section evaluated over a broad region of phase space, covering a wide range of inelasticity and photon virtuality. The gluon and sea contributions exhibit similar shapes, whereas the valence contribution, though relatively small, shows a markedly different behavior. The latter becomes particularly noticeable at large $x_{\mathbb{P}}$, a kinematic region not explored at HERA, but potentially accessible in future measurements at the Electron Ion Collider (EIC). This requires further feasibility studies. We also present the azimuthal angle modulation between the leptonic and the outgoing dijet planes for the general case, as well as for the ZEUS kinematic region where we see reasonable agreement with the data for diffractive deep inelastic scattering (DIS) parameter $β\gtrsim 0.4$.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a QCD analysis of exclusive dijet production in $e p \to e' jj p$ using GPDs in the DD approach, including contributions from quark and gluon exchanges for light and heavy quark pairs. It shows differential cross sections and azimuthal angle modulations, highlighting gluon and sea contributions dominance and valence quark's distinct behavior at large $x_P$, relevant for future EIC studies.
AI.motivation: To study exclusive dijet production in electron-proton collisions within the collinear QCD factorization framework, leveraging GPDs modeled via double distributions to understand contributions from various parton exchanges and predict observables for upcoming experiments like the EIC.
AI.method: Calculates cross sections using collinear QCD factorization with GPDs in the DD approach, incorporating all leading-order contributions from light sea/valence quarks and gluons for $q\bar{q}$ ($q=u,d,s$) and $c\bar{c}$ production. Differential distributions and azimuthal correlations are analyzed across varied kinematic regions.
AI.result: Gluon and sea contributions have similar shapes but dominate over valence quarks, which show different behavior, significant at large $x_P$ (unexplored at HERA). Azimuthal angle modulations agree with ZEUS data for $β ≥ 0.4$.
AI.conclusion: The work underscores the importance of gluon and sea quark contributions in dijet production, identifies valence quark effects as a discriminant in high $x_P$ regions accessible at EIC, and validates the model against existing ZEUS data, advocating for future feasibility studies.

title: Event-by-Event Multiplicity Fluctuations in Heavy-Ion Collisions Using Modified HIJING Monte Carlo Generator
summary: This work presents an analysis of event-by-event multiplicity fluctuations as a sensitive tool for diagnosing the state of matter produced in relativistic heavy-ion collisions. Using a modified version of the HIJING Monte Carlo generator, which integrates various models of partonic energy loss in hot (quark-gluon plasma) and cold media, the connection between fluctuation dynamics and system properties is investigated. It is shown that the nature and magnitude of fluctuations allow for the identification of the created medium type (hot or cold), the testing of the adequacy of energy loss models, and the detection of signatures of a first-order phase transition in different kinematic regions. The results obtained are important for interpreting the data from experiments aimed at mapping the QCD phase diagram and searching for the critical point.

AI.tldr: The paper examines event-by-event multiplicity fluctuations in heavy-ion collisions to identify the state of matter produced, test energy loss models, and detect signs of a first-order phase transition.
AI.motivation: To develop a diagnostic tool for understanding the state of the quark-gluon plasma and other media formed in heavy-ion collisions, validate energy loss models, and search for phase transitions relevant to the QCD phase diagram.
AI.method: A modified HIJING Monte Carlo model incorporating various partonic energy loss mechanisms in hot (quark-gluon plasma) and cold media is used to analyze multiplicity fluctuation patterns and their correlation with system properties.
AI.result: Fluctuation characteristics effectively distinguish between hot/cold media, assess energy loss model accuracy, and reveal potential signatures of a first-order phase transition in certain kinematic regions.
AI.conclusion: These fluctuation analyses provide critical insights for interpreting experimental data aimed at mapping the QCD phase diagram and locating the critical point.

title: First Estimation of Model Parameters for Neutrino-Induced Nucleon Knockout Using Simulation-Based Inference
summary: To enable an accurate determination of oscillation parameters, accelerator-based neutrino experiments require detailed simulations of nuclear interaction physics in the GeV regime. While substantial effort from both theory and experiment is currently being invested to improve the fidelity of these simulations, their present deficiencies typically oblige experimental collaborations to resort to empirical tuning of simulation model parameters. As the precision requirements of the field continue to become more stringent, machine learning techniques may provide a powerful means of handling corresponding growth in the complexity of future neutrino interaction model tuning exercises. To study the suitability of simulation-based inference (SBI) for this physics application, in this paper we revisit a tuned configuration of the GENIE neutrino event generator that was originally developed by the MicroBooNE collaboration. Despite closely reproducing the adopted values of four physics parameters when confronted with the tuned cross-section predictions as input, we find that our trained SBI algorithm prefers modestly different values (within MicroBooNE's assigned uncertainties) and achieves slightly better goodness-of-fit when inference is run on the experimental data set originally used by MicroBooNE. We also find that our trained algorithm can create a fair approximation of an alternative neutrino scattering simulation, NuWro, that shares only a subset of its physics model parameters with GENIE.

AI.tldr: This paper explores the use of simulation-based inference (SBI) to improve tuning of neutrino interaction models, demonstrating its ability to refine parameter estimation and approximate alternative simulation frameworks.
AI.motivation: Accelerator-based neutrino experiments rely on accurate nuclear interaction simulations, but current models require empirical tuning. With increasing precision demands, machine learning like SBI could better handle complex tuning tasks.
AI.method: The study revisits a tuned GENIE configuration from MicroBooNE. SBI is applied to cross-section predictions and experimental data. The algorithm's performance is evaluated by comparing inferred parameters and goodness-of-fit, and its ability to approximate NuWro is tested.
AI.result: The SBI algorithm finds parameter values slightly different from MicroBooNE's but within their uncertainties, with better goodness-of-fit. It also approximates NuWro's predictions despite differing parameters.
AI.conclusion: SBI shows promise for enhancing neutrino simulation tuning, offering improved parameter estimation and compatibility across different simulators, which is critical for upcoming high-precision experiments.

title: Heavy dibaryons $Ξ^{(*)}_{cc}Ξ^{(*)}_{cc}$ and $Ξ^{(*)}_{bb}Ξ^{(*)}_{bb}$
summary: We systematically investigate the dibaryons $Ξ^{(*)}_{cc}Ξ^{(*)}_{cc}$ (di-$Ξ_{cc}$) and $Ξ^{(*)}_{bb}Ξ^{(*)}_{bb}$ (di-$Ξ_{bb}$), with various isospin-spin configurations $I(J^P)$ in a nonrelativistic quark model. For the di-$Ξ_{cc}$ system, only the single channels $Ξ_{cc}Ξ^*_{cc}$ and $Ξ^*_{cc}Ξ^*_{cc}$ with $0(1^+)$ are capable of forming deuteronlike bound states, with the $σ$ meson exchange playing a decisive role. Those states have binding energies of approximately $-1.5$ MeV and $-3.3$ MeV and sizes of 2.37 fm and 1.87 fm, respectively. The coupled channel effect in the di-$Ξ_{cc}$ system with $0(1^+)$ enhances the attraction. As a result, this di-$Ξ_{cc}$ system can establish a deuteronlike configuration, with the binding energy of $-7.5$ MeV relative to the threshold $Ξ_{cc}Ξ_{cc}$ and the size of approximately 1.40 fm. For the di-$Ξ_{bb}$ system, the single channels with $0(1^+)$, $0(2^+)$, and $0(3^+)$ can give rise to deuteronlike bound states with binding energies ranging from $-6.1$ MeV to $-14.3$ MeV. Additionally, the di-$Ξ_{bb}$ system with $1(0^+)$ and $1(2^+)$ can also establish deuteronlike bound states with binding energies of around $-0.5$ MeV. When considering the coupled channel effect in the di-$Ξ_{bb}$ system with $0(1^+)$, a compact hexaquark state is formed, exhibiting a binding energy of $-21.2$ MeV relative to the threshold $Ξ_{bb}Ξ_{bb}$ and a size of 0.53 fm. In this state, the $π$ meson exchange provides a very powerful attractive force. The meson exchange interactions in the quark model is dispensable in the di-$Ξ_{bb}$ bound states, except for $Ξ_{bb}^*Ξ_{bb}^*$ with $1(0^+)$.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates dibaryon systems di-Ξ_{cc} and di-Ξ_{bb} using a nonrelativistic quark model, finding deuteron-like bound states with varying binding energies and sizes, driven by meson exchanges and coupled-channel effects.
AI.motivation: To systematically analyze the formation of dibaryons Ξ^{(*)}_{cc}Ξ^{(*)}_{cc} and Ξ^{(*)}_{bb}Ξ^{(*)}_{bb} with different isospin-spin configurations, focusing on their binding energies, sizes, and the role of meson exchanges in their interactions.
AI.method: A nonrelativistic quark model is applied to study the di-Ξ_{cc} and di-Ξ_{bb} systems. The analysis considers single and coupled channel effects, meson (σ and π) exchanges, and calculates binding energies and sizes for various isospin-spin configurations.
AI.result: For di-Ξ_{cc}: deuteron-like bound states in 0(1+) channels with binding energies of -1.5 and -3.3 MeV. Coupled channels enhance attraction, forming a state with -7.5 MeV binding and 1.40 fm. For di-Ξ_{bb}: multiple configurations form bound states with energies from -0.5 to -21.2 MeV. The 0(1+) coupled channel yields a compact hexaquark with -21.2 MeV and 0.53 fm due to π exchange.
AI.conclusion: Meson exchanges are crucial for binding, with σ dominating in di-Ξ_{cc} and π in di-Ξ_{bb} hexaquark. Coupled channels significantly enhance binding, enabling compact states. The results highlight differences in interaction mechanisms between charm and bottom dibaryons.

title: Subtracted Dispersion Relations for Virtual Compton Scattering off the Proton
summary: We present a once-subtracted dispersion relation (DR) formalism for the virtual Compton scattering (VCS) process from threshold up to the $Δ(1232)$ energy region. The formalism aims at extracting the nucleon's electric and magnetic generalized polarizabilities from the $e^- p \to e^- γp$ process, in view of the precision goals of the present and near future experiments at Jefferson Lab. The present work improves upon the existing unsubtracted DR formalism in several ways. The required $s$- and $t$-channel discontinuities in the subtracted dispersion integrals are obtained in a largely data-driven manner in this energy region. The $s$-channel dispersive integrals are reconstructed from $γ^\ast p \to πN \to γp$ using pion photo- and electro-production data, while the $t$-channel dispersion integrals are evaluated from $γ^\ast γ\to ππ\to N \bar N$ using recent dispersive analyses of both the $γ^\ast γ\to ππ$ and $ππ\to N \bar N$ processes. We compare our results to VCS data and show the sensitivity of the observables to the nucleon's scalar generalized polarizabilities, which enter the present formalism as subtraction constants.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an improved once-subtracted dispersion relation formalism for analyzing virtual Compton scattering (VCS) processes to extract nucleon's generalized polarizabilities, utilizing data-driven approaches for better precision in upcoming Jefferson Lab experiments.
AI.motivation: To enhance the precision of extracting nucleon electric and magnetic generalized polarizabilities by addressing limitations of existing unsubtracted dispersion relation methods, aiming for accuracy in current and future Jefferson Lab experiments.
AI.method:
Develops a once-subtracted dispersion relation formalism, reconstructing s- and t-channel discontinuities using pion photo/electro-production data and dispersive analyses of γ*γ→ππ and ππ→N
N processes to model VCS observables.
AI.result: Shows sensitivity of observables to nucleon scalar generalized polarizabilities (subtraction constants) and compares results with existing VCS data, demonstrating improved precision capabilities.
AI.conclusion: The formalism provides a robust data-driven approach to determine nucleon generalized polarizabilities with higher accuracy, critical for interpreting upcoming experimental data at Jefferson Lab.

title: Scattering observables and correlation function for $p ~f_1(1285)$ revisited
summary: In view of the recent theoretical developments in the fixed center approximation for the scattering of a particle with a a two-body cluster, implementing elastic unitarity on the standard fixed center formalism, and the imminent availability of ALICE data on the correlation function of the $p~f_1(1285)$ system, we update the results of a previous work for this correlation function and the low-energy scattering observables. The new results show appreciable changes in some observables and should provide valuable input for comparison with the forthcoming experimental data. Such a comparison is expected to yield relevant information on the nature of the axial-vector meson states.

AI.tldr: The paper updates previous results on the $p~f_1(1285)$ correlation function and low-energy scattering observables using an improved fixed center approximation method, which now includes elastic unitarity. The revised findings show significant changes in some observables and aim to provide a basis for comparing with upcoming ALICE experimental data to shed light on axial-vector meson states' nature.
AI.motivation: To incorporate recent theoretical advancements in the fixed center approximation by implementing elastic unitarity and utilize the soon-available ALICE data on $p~f_1(1285)$ correlations. This allows refining previous results and prepares for experimental validation.
AI.method: Updates the fixed center approximation method by enforcing elastic unitarity in the scattering framework. Applies this revised formalism to calculate the correlation function and scattering observables for the $p~f_1(1285)$ system.
AI.result: Key observables exhibit appreciable changes compared to prior work. The updated results are positioned to offer critical theoretical input for cross-checking with ALICE's upcoming experimental measurements.
AI.conclusion: The modified approach enhances the theoretical predictions' reliability, and the comparison with ALICE data will help clarify the axial-vector meson's properties, particularly regarding $f_1(1285)$'s nature.

title: Polarization transfer in $ψ'\toψππ$: a complete spin density matrix analysis framework
summary: A theoretical framework based on the Spin Density Matrix (SDM) formalism is developed to describe polarization transfer in the decay chain $e^+e^- \rightarrow ψ^\prime \rightarrow ψππ$. Explicit relations connecting the SDMs of $ψ^\prime$ and $ψ$ are derived, generalizing Cahn's analysis into a complete SDM treatment. For the dominant $S$-wave $ππ$ emission, the SDM is shown to be perfectly preserved, $ρ_ψ= ρ_{ψ^\prime}$, rendering the $ψ$ an ideal probe of the initial polarization state. Deviations arising from $D$-wave contributions are quantified, and a self-consistency experimental test is proposed that simultaneously validates the framework and constrains partial wave amplitudes. This formalism provides a consistent basis for extracting $ψ$ polarization and for amplitude analyses of subsequent $ψ$ decays in a continuum-background-free environment. The framework extends to other hadronic transitions, including $ψ' \to h_cπ^0$ in charmonium and $Υ(nS) \to Υ(mS)ππ$ in bottomonium, as well as to electroweak processes such as $e^+e^- \to Z^\ast \to ZH$, where the same angular-momentum structure governs polarization transfer -- offering a unified probe of dynamics from charmonium to the Higgs sector.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a Spin Density Matrix (SDM) framework to analyze polarization transfer in the decay chain $e^+e^- \rightarrow ψ' \rightarrow ψππ$. It establishes conserved SDM relations between $ψ'$ and $ψ$ under S-wave ππ emission, quantifies D-wave effects, and proposes experimental tests applicable to charmonium and bottomonium systems, as well as electroweak processes like $Z^* \to ZH$.
AI.motivation: To develop a rigorous SDM formalism extending Cahn's analysis for precise polarization transfer studies in multi-body decays, addressing theoretical gaps in handling hadronic transitions and continuum backgrounds. The motivation includes enabling accurate extraction of polarization parameters and unifying the treatment of diverse processes from quarkonia to Higgs physics.
AI.method: Derivation of SDM relations for the decay chain using angular momentum algebra, incorporating S- and D-wave ππ contributions. The method involves quantifying deviations from SDM conservation due to D-waves and formulating a self-consistency test for experimental validation. It also explores applicability across different particle systems and physics sectors.
AI.result: Confirmed SDM conservation in S-wave ππ emission, leading to ψ as a direct probe of initial polarization. Quantitative estimates of D-wave impacts on polarization transfer, and validated framework extending to other transitions like $ψ' \to h_cπ^0$ and $Υ(nS) \to Υ(mS)ππ$. The formalism unifies polarization analysis for charmonium, bottomonium, and electroweak processes.
AI.conclusion: The SDM framework provides a robust tool for high-precision polarization studies in hadronic and electroweak decays. It enables amplitude analyses with minimal background assumptions and offers a unified approach to explore dynamics across different energy scales, from heavy quarkonia to Higgs boson physics.

title: Jet energy loss in anisotropic plasmas meets limiting attractors
summary: We consider the energy loss of a high-energy parton in the early anisotropic plasma in heavy-ion collisions. Working in the harmonic approximation, we compute the change in the mean energy of an emitted gluon in the presence of an anisotropic background, characterized by anisotropic jet quenching parameters $\hat q_{x}\neq \hat q_{y}$. Evaluating the resulting integrals numerically, we compare with isotropic media, and obtain a simple pocket formula to estimate the impact of anisotropy on the mean emitted gluon energy, which is generally small. We then combine our results with the values of the jet quenching parameter extracted from QCD kinetic theory simulations and show that the medium length dependence of this mean energy loss exhibits the characteristics of limiting attractors, which can be obtained by extrapolating to zero and infinite coupling. Our study thus relates energy loss of jet partons to universal dynamics of anisotropic plasmas.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates energy loss of high-energy partons in anisotropic plasma from heavy-ion collisions, deriving a formula showing anisotropy's minor effect on gluon energy and finding medium length-dependent energy loss behavior resembling limiting attractors.
AI.motivation: To understand the impact of anisotropy in quark-gluon plasma on parton energy loss, extending beyond isotropic models for more accurate heavy-ion collision predictions.
AI.method: Using the harmonic approximation, the authors compute gluon energy changes in an anisotropic background with distinct $\hat q_x$ and $\hat q_y$ parameters, numerically evaluate integrals, and compare with isotropic cases. They utilize QCD kinetic theory simulation data for jet quenching parameters.
AI.result: Anisotropy produces a small effect on mean gluon energy. Medium length dependence of energy loss shows characteristics of limiting attractors, consistent with extrapolations at extreme couplings.
AI.conclusion: Anisotropic plasma energy loss dynamics align with universal attractor behavior, linking parton energy loss to fundamental anisotropic plasma properties.

title: Back-to-back dijet production in DIS with finite-energy corrections and twist-3 gluon TMDs
comment: 10 pages, proceedings of "School and Workshops on Elementary Particle Physics and Gravity" (CORFU2025)
summary: This work presents the summary of calculation of the cross section of the dijet production in deep inelastic scattering at small x at next-to-eikonal accuracy. The cross section is calculated in the back-to-back limit of the produced jets using results obtained in our previous works. The cross section is expressed via the transverse-momentum-dependent (TMD) parton distributions. Specifically, we show how the next-to-eikonal corrections are related to the $x$ dependent phase of twist-2 gluon TMD and to twist-3 unpolarized gluon TMDs.

AI.tldr: The study calculates dijet cross sections in deep inelastic scattering at small x with next-to-eikonal accuracy, linking corrections to gluon TMD phase and twist-3 distributions.
AI.motivation: To improve precision in high-energy scattering calculations by considering next-to-eikonal corrections previously unaccounted for.
AI.method: Uses TMD parton distributions and back-to-back jet limit, building on prior work to relate corrections to gluon TMD properties.
AI.result: Expressed cross section in terms of twist-2 gluon TMD phase and twist-3 unpolarized gluon TMDs, showing their role in next-to-eikonal contributions.
AI.conclusion: Next-to-eikonal effects are inherently connected to specific gluon TMD structures, enhancing our understanding of small-x scattering dynamics.
